1: <header>Techno-economic and environmental impact assessment of using corn stover biochar for manure derived renewable natural gas production </header>
Md Mosleh Uddin a , Zhiyou Wen b , Mark Mba Wright a,

<list>a Department of Mechanical Engineering, Iowa State University, Ames, IA 50011, United States b Department of Food Science and Human Nutrition, Iowa State University, Ames, IA 50011, United States</list>
2:
<list>A TEA and LCA of biochar-enhanced anaerobic digestion was conducted. 
Sensitivity and uncertainty analysis identified key parameters of the process. 
Costs of Renewable Natural Gas decreased by 12% with biochar use. 
RNG prices ranged from -$4.73 to $49/MMBTU. 
Biochar decreased RNG emissions from -0.038 to -0.043 gm/MJ.</list>

ARTICLEINFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Anaerobic Digestion Biochar Techno-economic analysis Life cycle assessment|As an additive to the Anaerobic Digestion (AD), biochar is attracting more research focused on improving digester performance and enhancing biogas yield. Despite the manifold positive impacts, literature data on economic viability and environmental impacts of biochar integration are still scarce. This study performs a comprehensive economic and environmental impacts analysis of biochar use in AD for Renewable Natural Gas (RNG) production. Corn stover biochar is used for the AD of beef manure, and biogas is upgraded to RNG. Process simulation models for AD, biogas upgrading, and RNG compression are developed in Aspen Plus TM The techno- economic analysis estimates that biochar addition reduces the minimum RNG selling price by 15% compared to the without biochar scenario. Digestate revenue and RIN credits would further lower the price by 22%. The increased biogas yield offsets the additional capital and feedstock costs for biochar. A cradle-to-gate life cycle assessment shows a 32% reduction in greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions for biochar addition when the biochar mixed with digestate is used for soil application. GHG reductions mostly come from the emission avoidance from conventional manure management and carbon sequestration with manure and biochar. Sensitivity and uncer- tainty analysis investigates the influence of different economic and process parameters on minimum selling price (MSP) and GHG emissions.
3: Livestock manure management produced 9.5% of the total for

Greenhouse Gas (GHG) of the agricultural sector in the USA in 2019 [1]. Anaerobic digestion technology help mitigate a significant amount of these GHG emissions while providing additional revenues from energy

production. Electricity produced from the AD biogas is not cost- competitive with inexpensive natural gas-based production. Upgrading biogas into Renewable natural gas (RNG), a drop-in alternative to nat- ural gas, has received attention due to its potential GHG emission reduction, fuel security, economic benefits, and better air quality [2]. Diversified use of RNG help decarbonize various GHG emitting sectors, especially the transportation sector when used as CNG (compressed natural gas) or LNG (liquified natural gas). A steady increase in RNG facilities has been visible in the USA since 2017 [3], mainly driven by available federal (Renewable fuel Standard Program) [4] and state-level (California low carbon fuel standard-LCFS, Oregon clean fuels program) incentives to produce RNG for vehicles transportation.

However, the widespread implementation of livestock manure- derived RNG production is still lacking due to higher up-front costs and technical barriers such as operational instability, process inhibition, and low biogas yield [5,6]. Extensive research has been conducted to overcome the technical barriers of AD, including feedstock pre- treatment, co-digestion, additive supplement, etc. [7]. Supplementing mineral nutrients, metal oxides, or enzymes enhanced digester stability and methane production [7,8].

A novel strategy of adding biochar in the AD digester has shown substantial improvement in process stability and biogas yield [6]. Bio- char is a carbonaceous material produced from the pyrolysis of biomass. Potential influences of biochar on AD include 1) mitigation of inter- mediate inhibitory products, 2) improvement of digester buffering ca- pacity, 3) improvement of syntrophic metabolism, 4) supporting microbial growth, and 5) improving digestate quality for soil applica- tions [9-12]. Argonne National Laboratory (ANL) published a series of studies on in-situ CO2 removal from biogas using biochar to attain nearly pipeline quality [13-16]. Additionally, biochar increases the holding capacity of digestate nutrients. Several researchers have reported that biochar enriched digestate provides a higher amount of organic matter and macro-micro nutrients to the soil [14,17].

A good number of experimental studies showed the positive impacts of biochar on the AD of animal manure. Kumar et al. [18] conducted the AD of cow dung with added commercial charcoal (5%, dry weight basis) for batch and semi-continuous processes. Biogas production increased by 17% and 34.7% for batch and semi-continuous processes. They found that higher microbial numbers for the charcoal amended substrate could increase the volatile solid degradation. Inthapanya and Preston [19] showed that adding 1% rice husk biochar to cattle manure increased the biogas production by 31% for a batch digester. Although the methane concentration was reduced by 8%, the overall methane production was increased by 27%. Jang et al. [20] used manure-derived biochar with dry dairy manure to investigate the effect on methane production for different biochar concentrations at different operating temperatures. A maximum methane yield was achieved for 10 g/L of biochar for all temperature levels. Methane yield was increased by 26.47%, 24.90%, and 24.69% at 20, 35, and 55 °C, respectively. They attributed the increased methane production to the lower VFA accumulation, espe- cially propionic acid, during AD. Gomez et al. [21] investigated AD's performance for converting swine manure with almond shell biochar under mesophilic conditions. Methane production increased by 39% compared to the mono digestion of swine manure. They proposed that biochar conductive properties enhanced cell connections, leading to higher protein degradation. Gong et al. [22] showed that activated carbon fiber as a biofilm carrier could enhance the biogas and methane production for the AD of cattle manure. Liang et al. [23] investigated the mesophilic AD of chicken manure and muskmelon leaf residue mixed with fruitwood biochar produced at 550 °C. They found an 18% increase in CH4 production. Experimental studies on the AD of non-manure substrates and various biochar sources are reviewed in several recent review papers [6,9-11].
Despite the growing number of experimental studies, biochar's economic and environmental impacts on cellulosic biomass-derived biogas have not been thoroughly investigated. Zhang et al.[24]

reported an economic feasibility study of biochar amended food waste AD, based on their pilot-scale plant in Singapore. They showed that the enhanced methane production exceeds the additional cost for biochar supplementation if the generated electricity is sold to the national grid. Salman et al. [25] performed a TEA (techno-economic analysis) of a novel biorefinery concept that added biochar to the AD of source- separated MSW. They have assumed a 5% increase in biomethane from AD for biochar addition as a conservative approach without detailed AD process modeling. The published literature does not clarify the trade-off between additional input cost and output energy benefit [26]. A recently published study investigated the economic profitability of biochar addition on the AD of orange peel waste for a plant processing 450,000 tons per year in a European context [27]. The study concluded that biochar addition to AD is economically unprofitable without sub- sidies higher than 20 EUR/MWh of biomethane for a biochar price of about 492 EUR per Mg. To the best of the authors' knowledge, no comprehensive economic and environmental impact analysis of biochar on animal manure-derived RNG production is available in the published literature.

The main objective of this study is to conduct a comprehensive Techno-Economic Analysis (TEA) to investigate the economic viability of biochar addition on cellulosic biomass-derived RNG production. TEA is a framework to evaluate the economic viability of any new technology or pathways using detailed costs, revenues, risk, and uncertainty anal- ysis. For this study, we have considered a case study of adding corn stover biochar to produce RNG from the AD of beef manure. Fig. 1 shows the block flow diagram of the studied pathway. This study answers the research question: does the increase in energy production justify the additional cost of biochar? As the environmental credits play a signifi- cant role in determining the economic viability of the RNG production, a life cycle assessment is conducted to quantify the net GHG emissions of this pathway. Sensitivity and uncertainty analysis investigates the bot- tlenecks and provides further research direction.

Sensitivity and uncertainty analysis investigates the bot- tlenecks and provides further research direction.
8: Livestock manure management produced 9.5% of the total for

Greenhouse Gas (GHG) of the agricultural sector in the USA in 2019 [1]. Anaerobic digestion technology help mitigate a significant amount of these GHG emissions while providing additional revenues from energy

production. Electricity produced from the AD biogas is not cost- competitive with inexpensive natural gas-based production. Upgrading biogas into Renewable natural gas (RNG), a drop-in alternative to nat- ural gas, has received attention due to its potential GHG emission reduction, fuel security, economic benefits, and better air quality [2]. Diversified use of RNG help decarbonize various GHG emitting sectors, especially the transportation sector when used as CNG (compressed natural gas) or LNG (liquified natural gas). A steady increase in RNG facilities has been visible in the USA since 2017 [3], mainly driven by available federal (Renewable fuel Standard Program) [4] and state-level (California low carbon fuel standard-LCFS, Oregon clean fuels program) incentives to produce RNG for vehicles transportation.

However, the widespread implementation of livestock manure- derived RNG production is still lacking due to higher up-front costs and technical barriers such as operational instability, process inhibition, and low biogas yield [5,6]. Extensive research has been conducted to overcome the technical barriers of AD, including feedstock pre- treatment, co-digestion, additive supplement, etc. [7]. Supplementing mineral nutrients, metal oxides, or enzymes enhanced digester stability and methane production [7,8].

A novel strategy of adding biochar in the AD digester has shown substantial improvement in process stability and biogas yield [6]. Bio- char is a carbonaceous material produced from the pyrolysis of biomass. Potential influences of biochar on AD include 1) mitigation of inter- mediate inhibitory products, 2) improvement of digester buffering ca- pacity, 3) improvement of syntrophic metabolism, 4) supporting microbial growth, and 5) improving digestate quality for soil applica- tions [9-12]. Argonne National Laboratory (ANL) published a series of studies on in-situ CO2 removal from biogas using biochar to attain nearly pipeline quality [13-16]. Additionally, biochar increases the holding capacity of digestate nutrients. Several researchers have reported that biochar enriched digestate provides a higher amount of organic matter and macro-micro nutrients to the soil [14,17].

A good number of experimental studies showed the positive impacts of biochar on the AD of animal manure. Kumar et al. [18] conducted the AD of cow dung with added commercial charcoal (5%, dry weight basis) for batch and semi-continuous processes. Biogas production increased by 17% and 34.7% for batch and semi-continuous processes. They found that higher microbial numbers for the charcoal amended substrate could increase the volatile solid degradation. Inthapanya and Preston [19] showed that adding 1% rice husk biochar to cattle manure increased the biogas production by 31% for a batch digester. Although the methane concentration was reduced by 8%, the overall methane production was increased by 27%. Jang et al. [20] used manure-derived biochar with dry dairy manure to investigate the effect on methane production for different biochar concentrations at different operating temperatures. A maximum methane yield was achieved for 10 g/L of biochar for all temperature levels. Methane yield was increased by 26.47%, 24.90%, and 24.69% at 20, 35, and 55 °C, respectively. They attributed the increased methane production to the lower VFA accumulation, espe- cially propionic acid, during AD. Gomez et al. [21] investigated AD's performance for converting swine manure with almond shell biochar under mesophilic conditions. Methane production increased by 39% compared to the mono digestion of swine manure. They proposed that biochar conductive properties enhanced cell connections, leading to higher protein degradation. Gong et al. [22] showed that activated carbon fiber as a biofilm carrier could enhance the biogas and methane production for the AD of cattle manure. Liang et al. [23] investigated the mesophilic AD of chicken manure and muskmelon leaf residue mixed with fruitwood biochar produced at 550 °C. They found an 18% increase in CH4 production. Experimental studies on the AD of non-manure substrates and various biochar sources are reviewed in several recent review papers [6,9-11].
Despite the growing number of experimental studies, biochar's economic and environmental impacts on cellulosic biomass-derived biogas have not been thoroughly investigated. Zhang et al.[24]

reported an economic feasibility study of biochar amended food waste AD, based on their pilot-scale plant in Singapore. They showed that the enhanced methane production exceeds the additional cost for biochar supplementation if the generated electricity is sold to the national grid. Salman et al. [25] performed a TEA (techno-economic analysis) of a novel biorefinery concept that added biochar to the AD of source- separated MSW. They have assumed a 5% increase in biomethane from AD for biochar addition as a conservative approach without detailed AD process modeling. The published literature does not clarify the trade-off between additional input cost and output energy benefit [26]. A recently published study investigated the economic profitability of biochar addition on the AD of orange peel waste for a plant processing 450,000 tons per year in a European context [27]. The study concluded that biochar addition to AD is economically unprofitable without sub- sidies higher than 20 EUR/MWh of biomethane for a biochar price of about 492 EUR per Mg. To the best of the authors' knowledge, no comprehensive economic and environmental impact analysis of biochar on animal manure-derived RNG production is available in the published literature.

The main objective of this study is to conduct a comprehensive Techno-Economic Analysis (TEA) to investigate the economic viability of biochar addition on cellulosic biomass-derived RNG production. TEA is a framework to evaluate the economic viability of any new technology or pathways using detailed costs, revenues, risk, and uncertainty anal- ysis. For this study, we have considered a case study of adding corn stover biochar to produce RNG from the AD of beef manure. Fig. 1 shows the block flow diagram of the studied pathway. This study answers the research question: does the increase in energy production justify the additional cost of biochar? As the environmental credits play a signifi- cant role in determining the economic viability of the RNG production, a life cycle assessment is conducted to quantify the net GHG emissions of this pathway. Sensitivity and uncertainty analysis investigates the bot- tlenecks and provides further research direction.

10: This study investigates two scenarios: I) without biochar - no biochar is mixed in the AD, II) with biochar biochar is mixed in the AD. A complete TEA is performed based on the material-energy balance data from the process simulation model and literature. The process simula- tion model for the AD of beef manure, biogas upgrading to RNG, compression, and pipeline injection are developed in Aspen Plus TM The RNG minimum-selling price (the break-even price to cover all manufacturing-related costs in addition to specified return on invest- ment) is calculated based on the Discounted Cash Flow Rate of Return (DCFROR) method. The influence of key processes and economic pa- rameters has been investigated using sensitivity analysis. Uncertainty in the RNG selling price from the key process and economic parameters has been quantified using the Monte Carlo simulation method. Life cycle assessment (LCA) investigates the Global Warming Potential (GWP) of resource and process emissions. A cradle-to-gate LCA is conducted using the USLCI database retrieved from SimaPro TM (Version 7.3.3) [28] software and literature data.
11: AD plants are divided into different technical areas: feedstock pre- processing, mixing, AD, byproduct separation, and biogas utilization CHP-combined heat and power, CNG, etc.) [29]. This study assumes a complete mix anaerobic digester due to its suitability for handling inhomogeneous feedstocks with high TS (up to 10%) at all climate conditions [30]. AD digester operating at mesophilic temperature range (35 °C) is convenient for complete feedstock digestion with a lower energy requirement than thermophilic digestion. The HRT of 40 days is chosen for this study [31]. The digester size is calculated by multiplying
Fig. 1. Block flow diagram of RNG production from anaerobic digestion of biochar amended manure.

the manure production rate by the required HRT. The heat to maintain digester temperature and the electricity for running ancillary equipment is provided by biogas combustion in a CHP engine. As described in the Results section, the developed kinetic process model gives manure's biochar enhanced biogas potential. A beef farm with 5000 cattle heads has been considered for this study. The beef manure production rate and characteristics employed in this study are listed in Table 1.

13: Biochar is produced mainly using three biomass carbonization pathways such as pyrolysis [33], gasification [34], and hydrothermal carbonization [35]. Pyrolysis is further categorized as slow pyrolysis and fast pyrolysis based on the pyrolytic temperature, heating rate, and residence time. Biochar yield is higher in slow pyrolysis (32% of biomass weight) than other pathways as their process parameters favor a higher yield of gaseous and liquid product [36]. As shown in Table 2, a wide variety of biochar has been tested with the AD of animal manure resulting in methane enhancement.

For this study, biochar is assumed to be produced from corn stover at a dedicated slow pyrolysis facility located at a 15 km distance from the facility. Corn stover is abundant in Iowa as an agricultural residue and can be easily collected within 15 km distance of a farm location. Based on Roberts et al. [38], an industrial-scale slow pyrolysis facility is considered. A swept drum kiln is used to pyrolyze corn stover at 450 °C through a continuous operation. The kiln is a highly airtight, horizontal cylindrical shell and externally heated. Biochar is fed after size reduction and moisture removal. Material and energy balance for the pyrolysis process and other process parameters are provided in the Supplemen- tary Information. The detailed economic analysis of the pyrolysis plant is beyond the scope of the study. The biochar price accounts for all costs associated with the biochar production in a pyrolysis facility.
Beef manure characteristics for anaerobic digestion [32].

A few infrastructural changes are necessary to facilitate biochar feeding to a digester. Biochar can be directly fed to the digester or mixed with the base substrate. Both cases would require an open-end loader to transfer biochar from the storage to the digester or mixing area before the digester. Biochar can be fed with separate feeding mechanisms, for example, screw conveyer or using the existing substrate feeding mech- anism. A magnetic separator may be necessary to remove any metallic element from the biochar to avoid digester toxicity. Controlling the biochar feed is crucial to prevent overdose, often leading to process inhibition. An automated weighing scale or conveyor belt scale is used for biochar feeding.

17: Biogas conversion to RNG requires scrubbing CO2, H2S, and other non-CH4 contaminants from raw biogas to meet commercial natural gas pipeline quality, known as upgrading. Most biogas upgrading technol- logies such as physical absorption, membrane separation, pressurized swing absorption, or cryogenic separation are technologically estab- lished to provide a high methane purity [39,40]. High-pressure water scrubbing (HPWS) has the highest number of installations around the world, being reliable, less complex, and easy-to-maintain [41,42]. Comparative techno-economic analysis shows slightly higher cost- effectiveness of HPWS for small plant size [41]. This technology is based on physical absorption mechanism where water absorbs CO2, H2S, and soluble contaminants from biogas based on Henry's law [39]. In an agricultural feedstock-based digester setting, HPWS is preferred as it
does not require any chemical processing, and water is usually abundant in agricultural farms [43].

Conventional biogas to RNG production may require H2S purifica- tion after the water scrubbing to ensure pipeline natural gas quality. However, biogas from biochar amended AD may not require this step due to the adsorption of H2S in the porous biochar structure. Shen et al. [16] reported that H2S concentration in biochar amended biogas is below the detection level (<5ppb), which is significantly lower than the US EPA pipeline-quality natural gas requirement of (<4 ppm). Kanja- narong et al.[44] also showed 98% H2S removal from biogas using a biochar column in a lab-scale CSTR. The produced RNG needs to be compressed to the pressure of the local natural gas grid. Thus, a gas compressor and a metering station are necessary for pipeline injection.

19: Rajendran et al. [45] developed an Aspen Plus TM library model for AD based on several kinetic reaction models [46,47]. The model has been validated by several industrial and experimental data with a 3-10% error [45]. Several researchers calibrated the model for different substrates and validated it with experimental results [48,49].

The process flow diagram of the AD library model is shown in Fig. 2. The NRTL (non-random two-liquid) method is used for the thermody- namic properties calculations. The model inputs are feedstock compo- sition, feedstock flow rate, hydraulic retention time (HRT), and digester temperature. The reaction rates are calculated using the power-law with kinetic parameters from different studies. A stoichiometric reactor (HYDREACT) contains hydrolysis reactions based on the fractional conversion factors of feedstock components. The monomers from the stoichiometric reactor flow to a continuously stirred tank reactor (CSTREACT). The CSTREACT contains acidogenesis, acetogenesis, and methanogenesis reactions. All reactions and kinetic parameters are given in ref [45]. Using the Fortran calculator blocks, the model calcu- lates kinetic parameters based on various inhibition factors such as NH3, pH, and long-chain fatty acid (LCFA). Readers are referred to ref [47] for the detailed descriptions of the calculator blocks.

This study modified the model by setting the kinetic reaction tem- peratures (in the power-law expressions) and reactors as 35 °C (meso- philic temperature range). A calculator block determines the input water flow to attain specific feedstock total solid (TS) content. The extent of the hydrolysis reactions is calibrated for manure digestion against the experimental data [31]. In the experiment, homogenized manure (with 95% moisture content) is anaerobically digested in a 10 L fermenter at 35 °C temperature for 40 days of HRT. The feedstock compositions for the experiment and simulation model are given in Table 3. The biogas flow rate from the simulation model is 1.5% higher than the experi- mental result, which is within the expected uncertainty range. The

The kinetic parameters of the model have been modified to incor- porate the effect of biochar addition on AD for the "with biochar" sce- nario. As discussed before, biochar addition increases digester buffering capacity due to its alkaline nature. The pH inhibition in the model is controlled by keeping a constant pH of 6.5. Biochar mitigates NH3 in- hibition by adsorbing free NH3 in its porous structure-NH3-inhibition is therefore removed from the model by modifying the Fortan calculator blocks. 25: The high-pressure water scrubbing (HPWS) process is simulated for biogas upgrading using the process simulation model from Cozma et al. [42] that has been validated with published literature. The process flow diagram of the HPWS is shown in Fig. 3. Biogas is pressurized to 10 bar using two compressors (COMP1, COMP2) and passed through the bot- tom of an absorption column (ABSORBER) packed with plastic media to achieve a large mass transfer surface. Freshwater flows through the top of the column and absorbs CO2, H2S, and other non-CH4 contaminants. The rich water containing CO2 and H2S is continuously withdrawn from the absorption column. Due to high pressure, the water also absorbs a small amount of CH4, causing "methane slip." The amount of CH4 loss depends on upgrading technologies, and for HPWS ranges from 1 to 3% [41]. For this study, 1% methane loss is assumed. A flash vessel is used to recover a portion of the absorbed CH4 and recirculate it to the compressor [40].

The rich water flows to a desorption column (STRIPPER), where the air is passed from the bottom at atmospheric pressure, and the CO2-rich gas is released at the top. The regenerated water is recycled to the ab- sorption column considering 5% water loss due to evaporation and leakage. The purified gas is then compressed by a multistage compressor (MULTICOM) and injected into the natural gas grid. The unit process parameters of the HPWS process are provided in the Supplementary Information.
26: The high-pressure water scrubbing (HPWS) process is simulated for biogas upgrading using the process simulation model from Cozma et al. [42] that has been validated with published literature. The process flow diagram of the HPWS is shown in Fig. 3. Biogas is pressurized to 10 bar using two compressors (COMP1, COMP2) and passed through the bot- tom of an absorption column (ABSORBER) packed with plastic media to achieve a large mass transfer surface. Freshwater flows through the top of the column and absorbs CO2, H2S, and other non-CH4 contaminants. The rich water containing CO2 and H2S is continuously withdrawn from the absorption column. Due to high pressure, the water also absorbs a small amount of CH4, causing "methane slip." The amount of CH4 loss depends on upgrading technologies, and for HPWS ranges from 1 to 3% [41]. For this study, 1% methane loss is assumed. A flash vessel is used to recover a portion of the absorbed CH4 and recirculate it to the compressor [40].

The rich water flows to a desorption column (STRIPPER), where the air is passed from the bottom at atmospheric pressure, and the CO2-rich gas is released at the top. The regenerated water is recycled to the ab- sorption column considering 5% water loss due to evaporation and leakage. The purified gas is then compressed by a multistage compressor (MULTICOM) and injected into the natural gas grid. The unit process parameters of the HPWS process are provided in the Supplementary Information.
27: Capital costs for the plant include the AD plant, water scrubbing process, RNG compression, and injection to the pipeline. The AD plant consists of several subsections: feedstock preprocessing unit, digester unit, gas collection and storage, digestate handling, and processing unit. The quoted cost for the AD plant typically includes the digester vessel and all other ancillaries. Capital costs for the digester and other

ancillaries are taken from Humbird et al. [52]. The costs of biochar feeding infrastructure (receiving station, open-end loader, conveyer belt) are obtained from published literature [53-55]. Equipment costs for the HPWS process are calculated based on Aspen Process Economic Analyzer (APEA) [56].

Different reports have published the RNG compressor, pipeline, and injection point construction costs. For this study, an RNG compressor is chosen with an output pressure of 3500 KPa [2]. Pipeline construction costs depend on location-specific factors such as distance between the plant and the injection point, pipeline pressure, construction condition, soil type, and right-of-way. An alternative solution was proposed by Murray et al. that assumes a third party would construct and maintain the distribution line. The biomethane facility would only pay a tariff for the transmission [57]. This approach would benefit the future bio- methane distribution plan for new facilities and reduce the economic uncertainty of the pipeline distance assumption. Given the absence of such policy in most states, this study assumes the biogas facility will construct a 3 mile of pipeline. The assumed pipeline construction costs are $ 428,333/mile [58] with a 4-inch diameter. The right-of-way for pipeline construction is equal to the high range estimate for North Carolina as a conservative point of reference [59]. The total capital cost for the meter station with gas monitoring and management would cost $1.3 million regardless of the gas injection volume.

Costs with different base years are converted to a 2019 basis using the Chemical Engineering Price Cost Index (CEPCI) value of 607.1 [60]. Equipment with different sizes is scaled to the required sizes along with cost conversion following Equation (1),
Required capacity n CEPCI in 2019 New cost = Base cost X X Base capacity CEPCI in base year (1)
Here, n is the scaling exponent to include the economy of scale in the cost calculations. For most equipment in this study, n = 0.6 is chosen [29].

Total project investment (TPI) is calculated as the sum of the total installed equipment costs (TIEC), over design allowance (ODA), con- tingency costs (CC), project management costs (PMC), and working capital (WC). TPI = TIEC + ODA + CC + PMC + WC (2)
Overdesign allowance, contingency costs, project management costs, and working capital are assumed to be 5%, 10%, 4%, and 5% of the TIEC, respectively.

30: Operating costs are categorized as variable operating costs and fixed operating costs. Variable operating costs depend on the production volume, whereas the fixed operating costs are constant throughout the
Fig. 3. Process flow diagram of high-pressure water scrubbing (HPWS) process.

operation period regardless of the production volume. Variable oper- ating costs include feedstock costs, consumables/chemical costs, utility (electricity, heating) costs, and feedstock transport costs. All variable costs are taken from a previous study [29] except biochar, electricity, and Renewable Identification Number (RIN) credits, as listed in Table 5. The only feedstock in this study is manure which is assumed to be purchased at $5/Mg [29]. Some researchers assumed zero costs for on- farm manure [54], and in many cases, tipping fees are assigned for processing manure from other farms [61]. The manure transport costs are not considered in the variable operating costs assuming on-farm plant location.

Biochar costs vary widely depending on the feedstock type, avail- ability, pyrolysis technology, and other region-specific parameters such as soil quality, crop production, biochar market, salary scale, [62]. No updated database is available to get the latest biochar market price for any region. In literature, various ranges are reported for a Mg of biochar such as $228-$280 (Poultry litter, Fast Pyrolysis-Gasifier) [63], $474-$704 (Wood, Auger pyrolysis) [64], $220-$280 (Pinewood, slow pyrolysis) [65], $75-248 (Rice, Fast pyrolysis), $680-1272 (Peanut, fast pyrolysis) [62], $218-274 (manure, mixed fast and slow pyrolysis) [66]. In their study, based on a European context, Arias et al. Field [27] considered a significantly higher biochar price ($546 per Mg 1). Brown et al. [67] estimated the minimum selling price of corn stover biochar for Iowa as $346/Mg biochar. Li et al. [62] provided an estimated price range between $87 and $250 for the same region. For this study, we consider the average of the higher range price estimate for corn stover biochar as $298/Mg. The price includes 15 km of transport costs of biochar from the pyrolysis facility to the RNG plant. A detailed cost analysis for biochar production is beyond this study's scope and is covered in detail in various previous articles [62,67,68].

The digestate selling price is a significant revenue stream to lower the RNG selling price. Solid-liquid separation is often preferred for easy handling and nutrient management if the digestate is sold as a fertilizer replacement. The liquid portion of the digestate is rich in nitrogen, whereas the solid portion is mostly phosphorus. Using digestate as fer- tilizer often requires additional processing to reduce pathogens, heavy metals, antibiotics used for animals, and other organic pollutants that might contaminate water and soil [69]. Moreover, excess nutrient sup- ply is a major challenge for sustainable agricultural practice. Applying unprocessed digestate for agriculture is not permitted in some EU countries, considering the risk of soil and water contaminations [7071]. In that case, getting rid of digestate can be an additional operational expense. The disposal costs depend on the location, soil nutrient con- ditions, available technologies, and governmental regulations for the disposal [72].

In this study, we have not considered any digestate disposal costs due a wide variation in available data. However, the minimum fuel selling price without digestate selling credits has been shown. For the base case scenario, $40/Mg and $2.64/Mg are considered the selling price of solid and liquid digestate, respectively [29].

The digester heat demand is provided by raw biogas combustion in a CHP (using a stationary internal combustion engine). However, natural from the grid is required to supply the start-up heat. Digester heat loss
Variable cost assumptions for the base case of manure anaerobic digestion to renewable natural gas.

Parameters|Value Plant financing by equity/debt|40%/60% of total capital investment Plant life|20 years Availability factor|85% Income tax rate|40% Interest rate for debt financing|7.5% Term of debt financing|10 years Working capital cost|5% of Fixed Capital Investment Depreciation schedule|7 years Construction period (spending schedule)|2.5 years (8% Y1, %Y2, 32 %Y3) Plant salvage value|None Start-up time|1 year Revenue and costs during start-up|Revenue = 50% of normal Revenue and costs during start-up|Variable cost = 75% of normal Revenue and costs during start-up|Fixed cost = 100% of normal

through walls and piping is also added to the total heat demand. The electricity for the pump, mixer, valves, and other ancillaries is assumed to be 12 kWh/Mg of feedstock [73]. Electricity demand for the com- pressors and pumps of the water scrubbing process and RNG compres- sion is calculated from the simulation model. The unit electricity price is 9.08 cents/kWh, based on the average retail price in Iowa in 2019 [74].

The US EPA assigns RIN (renewable identification number) credits to each gallon of renewable fuel. RNG produced from animal manure qualifies for D3 RIN credits (specific to cellulosic biofuel production). RINs can be sold in the market as a commodity to the obligated parties who need to blend a specific volume of renewable fuels, and the market price can vary widely over time. A $1.59/RIN is chosen as the base price for this study (November 2019) [75]. Another potential incentive is low carbon fuel standard (LCFS) credits administered by the California Air Resource Board (CARB) for supplying low carbon transportation fuel to California [76]. One LCFS credit is assigned to the producer for reducing 1 Mg of CO2 eq compared to the fossil-based fuel it replaces. CARB- approved dairy manure-derived RNG pathways have a wide variation in CI scores ranging from -177.03 to -532.74 [77]. The variations in CI score results from various factors such as feedstock production and conversion processes, utilization of byproducts, pipeline distance from the facility to California, business-as-us-usual manure management prac- tice, etc. To calculate the LCFS credit, this study assumes a CI score of -308.48, similar to an approved manure-derived RNG production project in Wisconsin [77]. As our cradle-to-gate LCA shows a 32% reduction in GHG emissions for the "with biochar" scenario, we consider the CI score 32% lower than the "without biochar" scenario. The LCFS credit price used in this study is $190.4/MT, the average price in 2021 (till October) [78]. Please note that this pathway has not been approved by CARB yet.

Fixed operating costs include annual employee salaries, overhead, maintenance, insurance, and taxes. The employee salaries are obtained from the Bureau of Labor Statistics [79]. The overhead expenses are assumed to be 5% of the total annual salaries. Annual maintenance and insurance costs are 2% of the total purchased equipment costs.

The project lifetime is considered 20 years with a 2.5-year con- struction period. Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS) is adopted to account for the capital depreciation for seven years of depreciation period. The key financial assumptions are listed in Table 6.

The goal of the LCA is to compare the environmental impact of beef manure-derived RNG for the "without biochar" and "with biochar" scenario, based on the attributional analysis. The guidelines provided by ISO 14040, 14,044 are followed [80,81]. Fig. 4 shows the system boundaries of the considered pathways with their inputs and outputs. All
Key financial assumptions for renewable natural gas production via anaerobic digestion.

Material|Costs Manure|$5/Mg Biochar|$298/Mg Solid digestate handling|$5/Mg Solid digested credit|$40/Mg Liquid digestate credit|$2.64/Mg Electricity|$0.09.08 RIN credits|$1.59/RIN LCFS credits|$190.4/credit
Fig. 4. System boundary of RNG production from animal manure, a) without biochar, b) with biochar (avoided processes are marked by dashed lines).
utilized inventory data are specific to the USA if not mentioned other- wise. The functional unit is defined as 1 MJ of RNG produced.

Following the cradle-to-gate approach, this study includes all emis- sions related to raw material extraction, transportation to plant, in-plant conversion, and delivery to the distribution point. The life cycle stages of the "without biochar" scenario include manure digestion, digestate transportation and application, biogas upgrading, RNG compression, and pipeline injection. The "with biochar" scenario includes biochar integrations related to lifecycle stages such as corn stover collection,
preprocessing, pyrolysis, and biochar application. The system bound- aries do not include emissions from capital goods, direct/indirect land- use change, and downstream RNG combustion.

2.4.2. Life cycle inventory (LCI) Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) data are collected from the USLCI database [82], published peer-reviewed literature, and process simulation model. LCA software SimaPro TM [28] is used to create the production system of RNG from beef manure. Two types of GHG emissions are considered for

the analysis: i) direct emissions (emissions produced from on-site ac- tivities and transportation), ii) indirect emissions (emissions produced from upstream or downstream processes outside the system boundary such as utilities).

2.4.2.1. Direct and indirect emission for manure only digestion. Direct emissions from an AD-RNG plant generally occur from fuel combustion for the digester start-up, biogas combustion for providing heat- electricity, biogas upgrading process, manure and digestate trans- portation, and digestate application machinery and soil after digestate application. Biogas combustion is generally considered carbon neutral as the emitted carbon was previously absorbed from the atmosphere. However, as we have fully credited the avoided emissions from con- ventional manure management, biogas combustion is considered a positive emission to avoid double counting. Fugitive CH4 emissions during AD and upgrading stages are considered 2% of the CH4 produced [73].

AD digestate applied to the soil emits CO2 and N20. In this study, all assumed parameters for CO2 and N2O emissions from digestate appli- cation are similar to Han et al. [73]. 62% of digestate C is assumed to be converted into CO2 over a 100-year time horizon, and the rest remains stored in the soil. N2O emissions occur via initrification-denitrification nitrate volatilization, and leaching/runoff to the water streams. N loss due to volatilization and leaching/runoff is considered to be 43% and 0.6%, respectively, similar to an anaerobic lagoon. N2O emission factors for both cases are 0.010 kg N2O-N/N loss. Indirect emissions include off- site production of electricity, natural gas, and diesel.

2.4.2.2. Avoided emissions for manure only digestion. Manure is usually collected from the farm, stored in a lagoon, and subsequently applied to the agricultural field. Emitted GHGs during manure storage are either burned or vented to the atmosphere, which is avoided if used for AD. The avoided emissions from the traditional manure management system are added as the credit, taken from Aui et al. [29]. The accounting of avoided emissions is in line with guidelines followed by government environmental agencies such as CARB [77] and the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).
The system expansion method is adopted to account for the digestate application. Synthetic N fertilizer is assumed to be replaced by the digestate N content.

2.4.2.3. Direct and indirect emissions related to biochar integration. Direct emissions result from corn stover collection, transportation, preprocess- ing, pyrolysis, biochar transportation to plant, and field transport. Emissions for corn stover collection and preprocessing are obtained from the GREET database based on ref [83]. A 15-km distance is assumed for the corn stover transport to the pyrolysis plant. The process parameters for an industrial-scale slow pyrolysis facility of corn stover are obtained from the study of Roberts et al. [38]. Material-energy balance and all process parameters are provided in the Supplementary Information. Emissions for biochar transportation for a 15-km travel radius are insignificant (9.64 X 10-6 kg CO2 eq/MJ of RNG) and hence excluded from the analysis. Indirect emissions include off-site natural gas, elec- tricity, and diesel production.

2.4.2.4. Avoided emissions for biochar integrated digestion. To the best of our knowledge, there is no published LCA data on the effect of biochar addition in AD for RNG production from manure. However, it is obvious that an increase in CH4 production due to biochar addition, on their own, would reduce emissions per functional unit.

We assume that the environmental benefits of biochar for soil application would be added to the benefit of manure digestate appli- cation. The N content of biochar could replace an equivalent amount of N fertilizer. However, most N would not be bioavailable due to the lower amount (0.5%) and its incalcitrant nature [15]. It has been reported that

biochar application reduces soil N2O emission and ammonium leaching in the long run due to the sorption capacity of biochar surfaces [84]. Biochar application can reduce 50% soil N2O emission from N fertilizer application [38]. Due to a lack of experimental data, a 1% reduction in soil N2O emission is considered for this study only to include the effect of biochar. This assumption is highly contingent on the biochar amount in digestate.

The significant contribution of biochar application is the sequestra- tion of biomass C into the soil, which was absorbed from the atmo- sphere. Due to biochar's molecular structure, most C becomes chemically and biologically stable and does not release CO2 over a long period (100-1000 years) when applied into the soil. For this study, 80% of the biochar C is considered stable, and the remaining 20% is emitted within the first year [38]. Moreover, the biochar conversion process (pyrolysis) produces syngas that replaces fossil natural gas for heating application. Further information on the LCI data is provided in the Supplementary Information.

Fugitive CH4 emissions during AD and upgrading stages are considered 2% of the CH4 produced [73].

AD digestate applied to the soil emits CO2 and N20. In this study, all assumed parameters for CO2 and N2O emissions from digestate appli- cation are similar to Han et al. [73]. 62% of digestate C is assumed to be converted into CO2 over a 100-year time horizon, and the rest remains stored in the soil. N2O emissions occur via initrification-denitrification nitrate volatilization, and leaching/runoff to the water streams. N loss due to volatilization and leaching/runoff is considered to be 43% and 0.6%, respectively, similar to an anaerobic lagoon. N2O emission factors for both cases are 0.010 kg N2O-N/N loss. Indirect emissions include off- site production of electricity, natural gas, and diesel.

2.4.2.2. Avoided emissions for manure only digestion. Manure is usually collected from the farm, stored in a lagoon, and subsequently applied to the agricultural field. Emitted GHGs during manure storage are either burned or vented to the atmosphere, which is avoided if used for AD. The avoided emissions from the traditional manure management system are added as the credit, taken from Aui et al. [29]. The accounting of avoided emissions is in line with guidelines followed by government environmental agencies such as CARB [77] and the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).
The system expansion method is adopted to account for the digestate application. Synthetic N fertilizer is assumed to be replaced by the digestate N content.

2.4.2.3. Direct and indirect emissions related to biochar integration. Direct emissions result from corn stover collection, transportation, preprocess- ing, pyrolysis, biochar transportation to plant, and field transport. Emissions for corn stover collection and preprocessing are obtained from the GREET database based on ref [83]. A 15-km distance is assumed for the corn stover transport to the pyrolysis plant. The process parameters for an industrial-scale slow pyrolysis facility of corn stover are obtained from the study of Roberts et al. [38]. Material-energy balance and all process parameters are provided in the Supplementary Information. Emissions for biochar transportation for a 15-km travel radius are insignificant (9.64 X 10-6 kg CO2 eq/MJ of RNG) and hence excluded from the analysis. Indirect emissions include off-site natural gas, elec- tricity, and diesel production.

2.4.2.4. Avoided emissions for biochar integrated digestion. To the best of our knowledge, there is no published LCA data on the effect of biochar addition in AD for RNG production from manure. However, it is obvious that an increase in CH4 production due to biochar addition, on their own, would reduce emissions per functional unit.

We assume that the environmental benefits of biochar for soil application would be added to the benefit of manure digestate appli- cation. The N content of biochar could replace an equivalent amount of N fertilizer. However, most N would not be bioavailable due to the lower amount (0.5%) and its incalcitrant nature [15]. It has been reported that

biochar application reduces soil N2O emission and ammonium leaching in the long run due to the sorption capacity of biochar surfaces [84]. Biochar application can reduce 50% soil N2O emission from N fertilizer application [38]. Due to a lack of experimental data, a 1% reduction in soil N2O emission is considered for this study only to include the effect of biochar. This assumption is highly contingent on the biochar amount in digestate.

The significant contribution of biochar application is the sequestra- tion of biomass C into the soil, which was absorbed from the atmo- sphere. Due to biochar's molecular structure, most C becomes chemically and biologically stable and does not release CO2 over a long period (100-1000 years) when applied into the soil. For this study, 80% of the biochar C is considered stable, and the remaining 20% is emitted within the first year [38]. Moreover, the biochar conversion process (pyrolysis) produces syngas that replaces fossil natural gas for heating application. Further information on the LCI data is provided in the Supplementary Information.
32: Operating costs are categorized as variable operating costs and fixed operating costs. Variable operating costs depend on the production volume, whereas the fixed operating costs are constant throughout the
Fig. 3. Process flow diagram of high-pressure water scrubbing (HPWS) process.

operation period regardless of the production volume. Variable oper- ating costs include feedstock costs, consumables/chemical costs, utility (electricity, heating) costs, and feedstock transport costs. All variable costs are taken from a previous study [29] except biochar, electricity, and Renewable Identification Number (RIN) credits, as listed in Table 5. The only feedstock in this study is manure which is assumed to be purchased at $5/Mg [29]. Some researchers assumed zero costs for on- farm manure [54], and in many cases, tipping fees are assigned for processing manure from other farms [61]. The manure transport costs are not considered in the variable operating costs assuming on-farm plant location.

Biochar costs vary widely depending on the feedstock type, avail- ability, pyrolysis technology, and other region-specific parameters such as soil quality, crop production, biochar market, salary scale, [62]. No updated database is available to get the latest biochar market price for any region. In literature, various ranges are reported for a Mg of biochar such as $228-$280 (Poultry litter, Fast Pyrolysis-Gasifier) [63], $474-$704 (Wood, Auger pyrolysis) [64], $220-$280 (Pinewood, slow pyrolysis) [65], $75-248 (Rice, Fast pyrolysis), $680-1272 (Peanut, fast pyrolysis) [62], $218-274 (manure, mixed fast and slow pyrolysis) [66]. In their study, based on a European context, Arias et al. Field [27] considered a significantly higher biochar price ($546 per Mg 1). Brown et al. [67] estimated the minimum selling price of corn stover biochar for Iowa as $346/Mg biochar. Li et al. [62] provided an estimated price range between $87 and $250 for the same region. For this study, we consider the average of the higher range price estimate for corn stover biochar as $298/Mg. The price includes 15 km of transport costs of biochar from the pyrolysis facility to the RNG plant. A detailed cost analysis for biochar production is beyond this study's scope and is covered in detail in various previous articles [62,67,68].

The digestate selling price is a significant revenue stream to lower the RNG selling price. Solid-liquid separation is often preferred for easy handling and nutrient management if the digestate is sold as a fertilizer replacement. The liquid portion of the digestate is rich in nitrogen, whereas the solid portion is mostly phosphorus. Using digestate as fer- tilizer often requires additional processing to reduce pathogens, heavy metals, antibiotics used for animals, and other organic pollutants that might contaminate water and soil [69]. Moreover, excess nutrient sup- ply is a major challenge for sustainable agricultural practice. Applying unprocessed digestate for agriculture is not permitted in some EU countries, considering the risk of soil and water contaminations [7071]. In that case, getting rid of digestate can be an additional operational expense. The disposal costs depend on the location, soil nutrient con- ditions, available technologies, and governmental regulations for the disposal [72].

In this study, we have not considered any digestate disposal costs due a wide variation in available data. However, the minimum fuel selling price without digestate selling credits has been shown. For the base case scenario, $40/Mg and $2.64/Mg are considered the selling price of solid and liquid digestate, respectively [29].

The digester heat demand is provided by raw biogas combustion in a CHP (using a stationary internal combustion engine). However, natural from the grid is required to supply the start-up heat. Digester heat loss
Variable cost assumptions for the base case of manure anaerobic digestion to renewable natural gas.

Parameters|Value Plant financing by equity/debt|40%/60% of total capital investment Plant life|20 years Availability factor|85% Income tax rate|40% Interest rate for debt financing|7.5% Term of debt financing|10 years Working capital cost|5% of Fixed Capital Investment Depreciation schedule|7 years Construction period (spending schedule)|2.5 years (8% Y1, %Y2, 32 %Y3) Plant salvage value|None Start-up time|1 year Revenue and costs during start-up|Revenue = 50% of normal Revenue and costs during start-up|Variable cost = 75% of normal Revenue and costs during start-up|Fixed cost = 100% of normal

through walls and piping is also added to the total heat demand. The electricity for the pump, mixer, valves, and other ancillaries is assumed to be 12 kWh/Mg of feedstock [73]. Electricity demand for the com- pressors and pumps of the water scrubbing process and RNG compres- sion is calculated from the simulation model. The unit electricity price is 9.08 cents/kWh, based on the average retail price in Iowa in 2019 [74].

The US EPA assigns RIN (renewable identification number) credits to each gallon of renewable fuel. RNG produced from animal manure qualifies for D3 RIN credits (specific to cellulosic biofuel production). RINs can be sold in the market as a commodity to the obligated parties who need to blend a specific volume of renewable fuels, and the market price can vary widely over time. A $1.59/RIN is chosen as the base price for this study (November 2019) [75]. Another potential incentive is low carbon fuel standard (LCFS) credits administered by the California Air Resource Board (CARB) for supplying low carbon transportation fuel to California [76]. One LCFS credit is assigned to the producer for reducing 1 Mg of CO2 eq compared to the fossil-based fuel it replaces. CARB- approved dairy manure-derived RNG pathways have a wide variation in CI scores ranging from -177.03 to -532.74 [77]. The variations in CI score results from various factors such as feedstock production and conversion processes, utilization of byproducts, pipeline distance from the facility to California, business-as-us-usual manure management prac- tice, etc. To calculate the LCFS credit, this study assumes a CI score of -308.48, similar to an approved manure-derived RNG production project in Wisconsin [77]. As our cradle-to-gate LCA shows a 32% reduction in GHG emissions for the "with biochar" scenario, we consider the CI score 32% lower than the "without biochar" scenario. The LCFS credit price used in this study is $190.4/MT, the average price in 2021 (till October) [78]. Please note that this pathway has not been approved by CARB yet.

Fixed operating costs include annual employee salaries, overhead, maintenance, insurance, and taxes. The employee salaries are obtained from the Bureau of Labor Statistics [79]. The overhead expenses are assumed to be 5% of the total annual salaries. Annual maintenance and insurance costs are 2% of the total purchased equipment costs.

The project lifetime is considered 20 years with a 2.5-year con- struction period. Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS) is adopted to account for the capital depreciation for seven years of depreciation period. The key financial assumptions are listed in Table 6.

The goal of the LCA is to compare the environmental impact of beef manure-derived RNG for the "without biochar" and "with biochar" scenario, based on the attributional analysis. The guidelines provided by ISO 14040, 14,044 are followed [80,81]. Fig. 4 shows the system boundaries of the considered pathways with their inputs and outputs. All
Key financial assumptions for renewable natural gas production via anaerobic digestion.

Material|Costs Manure|$5/Mg Biochar|$298/Mg Solid digestate handling|$5/Mg Solid digested credit|$40/Mg Liquid digestate credit|$2.64/Mg Electricity|$0.09.08 RIN credits|$1.59/RIN LCFS credits|$190.4/credit
Fig. 4. System boundary of RNG production from animal manure, a) without biochar, b) with biochar (avoided processes are marked by dashed lines).
utilized inventory data are specific to the USA if not mentioned other- wise. The functional unit is defined as 1 MJ of RNG produced.

Following the cradle-to-gate approach, this study includes all emis- sions related to raw material extraction, transportation to plant, in-plant conversion, and delivery to the distribution point. The life cycle stages of the "without biochar" scenario include manure digestion, digestate transportation and application, biogas upgrading, RNG compression, and pipeline injection. The "with biochar" scenario includes biochar integrations related to lifecycle stages such as corn stover collection,
preprocessing, pyrolysis, and biochar application. The system bound- aries do not include emissions from capital goods, direct/indirect land- use change, and downstream RNG combustion.

2.4.2. Life cycle inventory (LCI) Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) data are collected from the USLCI database [82], published peer-reviewed literature, and process simulation model. LCA software SimaPro TM [28] is used to create the production system of RNG from beef manure. Two types of GHG emissions are considered for

the analysis: i) direct emissions (emissions produced from on-site ac- tivities and transportation), ii) indirect emissions (emissions produced from upstream or downstream processes outside the system boundary such as utilities).

In their study, based on a European context, Arias et al. Field [27] considered a significantly higher biochar price ($546 per Mg 1). Brown et al. [67] estimated the minimum selling price of corn stover biochar for Iowa as $346/Mg biochar. Li et al. [62] provided an estimated price range between $87 and $250 for the same region. For this study, we consider the average of the higher range price estimate for corn stover biochar as $298/Mg. The price includes 15 km of transport costs of biochar from the pyrolysis facility to the RNG plant. A detailed cost analysis for biochar production is beyond this study's scope and is covered in detail in various previous articles [62,67,68].

The digestate selling price is a significant revenue stream to lower the RNG selling price. Solid-liquid separation is often preferred for easy handling and nutrient management if the digestate is sold as a fertilizer replacement. The liquid portion of the digestate is rich in nitrogen, whereas the solid portion is mostly phosphorus. Using digestate as fer- tilizer often requires additional processing to reduce pathogens, heavy metals, antibiotics used for animals, and other organic pollutants that might contaminate water and soil [69]. Moreover, excess nutrient sup- ply is a major challenge for sustainable agricultural practice. Applying unprocessed digestate for agriculture is not permitted in some EU countries, considering the risk of soil and water contaminations [7071]. In that case, getting rid of digestate can be an additional operational expense. The disposal costs depend on the location, soil nutrient con- ditions, available technologies, and governmental regulations for the disposal [72].

In this study, we have not considered any digestate disposal costs due a wide variation in available data. However, the minimum fuel selling price without digestate selling credits has been shown. For the base case scenario, $40/Mg and $2.64/Mg are considered the selling price of solid and liquid digestate, respectively [29].

The digester heat demand is provided by raw biogas combustion in a CHP (using a stationary internal combustion engine). However, natural from the grid is required to supply the start-up heat. Digester heat loss
Variable cost assumptions for the base case of manure anaerobic digestion to renewable natural gas.

Parameters|Value Plant financing by equity/debt|40%/60% of total capital investment Plant life|20 years Availability factor|85% Income tax rate|40% Interest rate for debt financing|7.5% Term of debt financing|10 years Working capital cost|5% of Fixed Capital Investment Depreciation schedule|7 years Construction period (spending schedule)|2.5 years (8% Y1, %Y2, 32 %Y3) Plant salvage value|None Start-up time|1 year Revenue and costs during start-up|Revenue = 50% of normal Revenue and costs during start-up|Variable cost = 75% of normal Revenue and costs during start-up|Fixed cost = 100% of normal

through walls and piping is also added to the total heat demand. The electricity for the pump, mixer, valves, and other ancillaries is assumed to be 12 kWh/Mg of feedstock [73]. Electricity demand for the com- pressors and pumps of the water scrubbing process and RNG compres- sion is calculated from the simulation model. The unit electricity price is 9.08 cents/kWh, based on the average retail price in Iowa in 2019 [74].

The US EPA assigns RIN (renewable identification number) credits to each gallon of renewable fuel. RNG produced from animal manure qualifies for D3 RIN credits (specific to cellulosic biofuel production). RINs can be sold in the market as a commodity to the obligated parties who need to blend a specific volume of renewable fuels, and the market price can vary widely over time. A $1.59/RIN is chosen as the base price for this study (November 2019) [75]. Another potential incentive is low carbon fuel standard (LCFS) credits administered by the California Air Resource Board (CARB) for supplying low carbon transportation fuel to California [76]. One LCFS credit is assigned to the producer for reducing 1 Mg of CO2 eq compared to the fossil-based fuel it replaces. CARB- approved dairy manure-derived RNG pathways have a wide variation in CI scores ranging from -177.03 to -532.74 [77]. The variations in CI score results from various factors such as feedstock production and conversion processes, utilization of byproducts, pipeline distance from the facility to California, business-as-us-usual manure management prac- tice, etc. To calculate the LCFS credit, this study assumes a CI score of -308.48, similar to an approved manure-derived RNG production project in Wisconsin [77]. As our cradle-to-gate LCA shows a 32% reduction in GHG emissions for the "with biochar" scenario, we consider the CI score 32% lower than the "without biochar" scenario. The LCFS credit price used in this study is $190.4/MT, the average price in 2021 (till October) [78]. Please note that this pathway has not been approved by CARB yet.

Fixed operating costs include annual employee salaries, overhead, maintenance, insurance, and taxes. The employee salaries are obtained from the Bureau of Labor Statistics [79]. The overhead expenses are assumed to be 5% of the total annual salaries. Annual maintenance and insurance costs are 2% of the total purchased equipment costs.

The project lifetime is considered 20 years with a 2.5-year con- struction period. Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS) is adopted to account for the capital depreciation for seven years of depreciation period. The key financial assumptions are listed in Table 6.

The goal of the LCA is to compare the environmental impact of beef manure-derived RNG for the "without biochar" and "with biochar" scenario, based on the attributional analysis. The guidelines provided by ISO 14040, 14,044 are followed [80,81]. Fig. 4 shows the system boundaries of the considered pathways with their inputs and outputs. All

Material|Costs Manure|$5/Mg Biochar|$298/Mg Solid digestate handling|$5/Mg Solid digested credit|$40/Mg Liquid digestate credit|$2.64/Mg Electricity|$0.09.08 RIN credits|$1.59/RIN LCFS credits|$190.4/credit

utilized inventory data are specific to the USA if not mentioned other- wise. The functional unit is defined as 1 MJ of RNG produced.

Following the cradle-to-gate approach, this study includes all emis- sions related to raw material extraction, transportation to plant, in-plant conversion, and delivery to the distribution point. The life cycle stages of the "without biochar" scenario include manure digestion, digestate transportation and application, biogas upgrading, RNG compression, and pipeline injection. The "with biochar" scenario includes biochar integrations related to lifecycle stages such as corn stover collection,
preprocessing, pyrolysis, and biochar application. The system bound- aries do not include emissions from capital goods, direct/indirect land- use change, and downstream RNG combustion.

2.4.2. Life cycle inventory (LCI) Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) data are collected from the USLCI database [82], published peer-reviewed literature, and process simulation model. LCA software SimaPro TM [28] is used to create the production system of RNG from beef manure. Two types of GHG emissions are considered for

the analysis: i) direct emissions (emissions produced from on-site ac- tivities and transportation), ii) indirect emissions (emissions produced from upstream or downstream processes outside the system boundary such as utilities).

2.4.2.1. Direct and indirect emission for manure only digestion. Direct emissions from an AD-RNG plant generally occur from fuel combustion for the digester start-up, biogas combustion for providing heat- electricity, biogas upgrading process, manure and digestate trans- portation, and digestate application machinery and soil after digestate application. Biogas combustion is generally considered carbon neutral as the emitted carbon was previously absorbed from the atmosphere. However, as we have fully credited the avoided emissions from con- ventional manure management, biogas combustion is considered a positive emission to avoid double counting. Fugitive CH4 emissions during AD and upgrading stages are considered 2% of the CH4 produced [73].

AD digestate applied to the soil emits CO2 and N20. In this study, all assumed parameters for CO2 and N2O emissions from digestate appli- cation are similar to Han et al. [73]. 62% of digestate C is assumed to be converted into CO2 over a 100-year time horizon, and the rest remains stored in the soil. N2O emissions occur via initrification-denitrification nitrate volatilization, and leaching/runoff to the water streams. N loss due to volatilization and leaching/runoff is considered to be 43% and 0.6%, respectively, similar to an anaerobic lagoon. N2O emission factors for both cases are 0.010 kg N2O-N/N loss. Indirect emissions include off- site production of electricity, natural gas, and diesel.

2.4.2.2. Avoided emissions for manure only digestion. Manure is usually collected from the farm, stored in a lagoon, and subsequently applied to the agricultural field. Emitted GHGs during manure storage are either burned or vented to the atmosphere, which is avoided if used for AD. The avoided emissions from the traditional manure management system are added as the credit, taken from Aui et al. [29]. The accounting of avoided emissions is in line with guidelines followed by government environmental agencies such as CARB [77] and the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).
The system expansion method is adopted to account for the digestate application. Synthetic N fertilizer is assumed to be replaced by the digestate N content.

2.4.2.3. Direct and indirect emissions related to biochar integration. Direct emissions result from corn stover collection, transportation, preprocess- ing, pyrolysis, biochar transportation to plant, and field transport. Emissions for corn stover collection and preprocessing are obtained from the GREET database based on ref [83]. A 15-km distance is assumed for the corn stover transport to the pyrolysis plant. The process parameters for an industrial-scale slow pyrolysis facility of corn stover are obtained from the study of Roberts et al. [38]. Material-energy balance and all process parameters are provided in the Supplementary Information. Emissions for biochar transportation for a 15-km travel radius are insignificant (9.64 X 10-6 kg CO2 eq/MJ of RNG) and hence excluded from the analysis. Indirect emissions include off-site natural gas, elec- tricity, and diesel production.

2.4.2.4. Avoided emissions for biochar integrated digestion. To the best of our knowledge, there is no published LCA data on the effect of biochar addition in AD for RNG production from manure. However, it is obvious that an increase in CH4 production due to biochar addition, on their own, would reduce emissions per functional unit.

We assume that the environmental benefits of biochar for soil application would be added to the benefit of manure digestate appli- cation. The N content of biochar could replace an equivalent amount of N fertilizer. However, most N would not be bioavailable due to the lower amount (0.5%) and its incalcitrant nature [15]. It has been reported that

biochar application reduces soil N2O emission and ammonium leaching in the long run due to the sorption capacity of biochar surfaces [84]. Biochar application can reduce 50% soil N2O emission from N fertilizer application [38]. Due to a lack of experimental data, a 1% reduction in soil N2O emission is considered for this study only to include the effect of biochar. This assumption is highly contingent on the biochar amount in digestate.

The significant contribution of biochar application is the sequestra- tion of biomass C into the soil, which was absorbed from the atmo- sphere. Due to biochar's molecular structure, most C becomes chemically and biologically stable and does not release CO2 over a long period (100-1000 years) when applied into the soil. For this study, 80% of the biochar C is considered stable, and the remaining 20% is emitted within the first year [38]. Moreover, the biochar conversion process (pyrolysis) produces syngas that replaces fossil natural gas for heating application. Further information on the LCI data is provided in the Supplementary Information.

The project lifetime is considered 20 years with a 2.5-year con- struction period. Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS) is adopted to account for the capital depreciation for seven years of depreciation period. The key financial assumptions are listed in Table 6.

The goal of the LCA is to compare the environmental impact of beef manure-derived RNG for the "without biochar" and "with biochar" scenario, based on the attributional analysis. The guidelines provided by ISO 14040, 14,044 are followed [80,81]. Fig. 4 shows the system boundaries of the considered pathways with their inputs and outputs. All

Material|Costs Manure|$5/Mg Biochar|$298/Mg Solid digestate handling|$5/Mg Solid digested credit|$40/Mg Liquid digestate credit|$2.64/Mg Electricity|$0.09.08 RIN credits|$1.59/RIN LCFS credits|$190.4/credit
