utilized inventory data are specific to the USA if not mentioned other- wise. The functional unit is defined as 1 MJ of RNG produced.

Following the cradle-to-gate approach, this study includes all emis- sions related to raw material extraction, transportation to plant, in-plant conversion, and delivery to the distribution point. The life cycle stages of the "without biochar" scenario include manure digestion, digestate transportation and application, biogas upgrading, RNG compression, and pipeline injection. The "with biochar" scenario includes biochar integrations related to lifecycle stages such as corn stover collection,
preprocessing, pyrolysis, and biochar application. The system bound- aries do not include emissions from capital goods, direct/indirect land- use change, and downstream RNG combustion.

2.4.2. Life cycle inventory (LCI) Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) data are collected from the USLCI database [82], published peer-reviewed literature, and process simulation model. LCA software SimaPro TM [28] is used to create the production system of RNG from beef manure. Two types of GHG emissions are considered for

the analysis: i) direct emissions (emissions produced from on-site ac- tivities and transportation), ii) indirect emissions (emissions produced from upstream or downstream processes outside the system boundary such as utilities).

2.4.2.1. Direct and indirect emission for manure only digestion. Direct emissions from an AD-RNG plant generally occur from fuel combustion for the digester start-up, biogas combustion for providing heat- electricity, biogas upgrading process, manure and digestate trans- portation, and digestate application machinery and soil after digestate application. Biogas combustion is generally considered carbon neutral as the emitted carbon was previously absorbed from the atmosphere. However, as we have fully credited the avoided emissions from con- ventional manure management, biogas combustion is considered a positive emission to avoid double counting. Fugitive CH4 emissions during AD and upgrading stages are considered 2% of the CH4 produced [73].

AD digestate applied to the soil emits CO2 and N20. In this study, all assumed parameters for CO2 and N2O emissions from digestate appli- cation are similar to Han et al. [73]. 62% of digestate C is assumed to be converted into CO2 over a 100-year time horizon, and the rest remains stored in the soil. N2O emissions occur via initrification-denitrification nitrate volatilization, and leaching/runoff to the water streams. N loss due to volatilization and leaching/runoff is considered to be 43% and 0.6%, respectively, similar to an anaerobic lagoon. N2O emission factors for both cases are 0.010 kg N2O-N/N loss. Indirect emissions include off- site production of electricity, natural gas, and diesel.

2.4.2.2. Avoided emissions for manure only digestion. Manure is usually collected from the farm, stored in a lagoon, and subsequently applied to the agricultural field. Emitted GHGs during manure storage are either burned or vented to the atmosphere, which is avoided if used for AD. The avoided emissions from the traditional manure management system are added as the credit, taken from Aui et al. [29]. The accounting of avoided emissions is in line with guidelines followed by government environmental agencies such as CARB [77] and the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).
The system expansion method is adopted to account for the digestate application. Synthetic N fertilizer is assumed to be replaced by the digestate N content.

2.4.2.3. Direct and indirect emissions related to biochar integration. Direct emissions result from corn stover collection, transportation, preprocess- ing, pyrolysis, biochar transportation to plant, and field transport. Emissions for corn stover collection and preprocessing are obtained from the GREET database based on ref [83]. A 15-km distance is assumed for the corn stover transport to the pyrolysis plant. The process parameters for an industrial-scale slow pyrolysis facility of corn stover are obtained from the study of Roberts et al. [38]. Material-energy balance and all process parameters are provided in the Supplementary Information. Emissions for biochar transportation for a 15-km travel radius are insignificant (9.64 X 10-6 kg CO2 eq/MJ of RNG) and hence excluded from the analysis. Indirect emissions include off-site natural gas, elec- tricity, and diesel production.

2.4.2.4. Avoided emissions for biochar integrated digestion. To the best of our knowledge, there is no published LCA data on the effect of biochar addition in AD for RNG production from manure. However, it is obvious that an increase in CH4 production due to biochar addition, on their own, would reduce emissions per functional unit.

We assume that the environmental benefits of biochar for soil application would be added to the benefit of manure digestate appli- cation. The N content of biochar could replace an equivalent amount of N fertilizer. However, most N would not be bioavailable due to the lower amount (0.5%) and its incalcitrant nature [15]. It has been reported that

biochar application reduces soil N2O emission and ammonium leaching in the long run due to the sorption capacity of biochar surfaces [84]. Biochar application can reduce 50% soil N2O emission from N fertilizer application [38]. Due to a lack of experimental data, a 1% reduction in soil N2O emission is considered for this study only to include the effect of biochar. This assumption is highly contingent on the biochar amount in digestate.

The significant contribution of biochar application is the sequestra- tion of biomass C into the soil, which was absorbed from the atmo- sphere. Due to biochar's molecular structure, most C becomes chemically and biologically stable and does not release CO2 over a long period (100-1000 years) when applied into the soil. For this study, 80% of the biochar C is considered stable, and the remaining 20% is emitted within the first year [38]. Moreover, the biochar conversion process (pyrolysis) produces syngas that replaces fossil natural gas for heating application. Further information on the LCI data is provided in the Supplementary Information.
37: Operating costs are categorized as variable operating costs and fixed operating costs. Variable operating costs depend on the production volume, whereas the fixed operating costs are constant throughout the
Fig. 3. Process flow diagram of high-pressure water scrubbing (HPWS) process.

operation period regardless of the production volume. Variable oper- ating costs include feedstock costs, consumables/chemical costs, utility (electricity, heating) costs, and feedstock transport costs. All variable costs are taken from a previous study [29] except biochar, electricity, and Renewable Identification Number (RIN) credits, as listed in Table 5. The only feedstock in this study is manure which is assumed to be purchased at $5/Mg [29]. Some researchers assumed zero costs for on- farm manure [54], and in many cases, tipping fees are assigned for processing manure from other farms [61]. The manure transport costs are not considered in the variable operating costs assuming on-farm plant location.

Biochar costs vary widely depending on the feedstock type, avail- ability, pyrolysis technology, and other region-specific parameters such as soil quality, crop production, biochar market, salary scale, [62]. No updated database is available to get the latest biochar market price for any region. In literature, various ranges are reported for a Mg of biochar such as $228-$280 (Poultry litter, Fast Pyrolysis-Gasifier) [63], $474-$704 (Wood, Auger pyrolysis) [64], $220-$280 (Pinewood, slow pyrolysis) [65], $75-248 (Rice, Fast pyrolysis), $680-1272 (Peanut, fast pyrolysis) [62], $218-274 (manure, mixed fast and slow pyrolysis) [66]. In their study, based on a European context, Arias et al. Field [27] considered a significantly higher biochar price ($546 per Mg 1). Brown et al. [67] estimated the minimum selling price of corn stover biochar for Iowa as $346/Mg biochar. Li et al. [62] provided an estimated price range between $87 and $250 for the same region. For this study, we consider the average of the higher range price estimate for corn stover biochar as $298/Mg. The price includes 15 km of transport costs of biochar from the pyrolysis facility to the RNG plant. A detailed cost analysis for biochar production is beyond this study's scope and is covered in detail in various previous articles [62,67,68].

The digestate selling price is a significant revenue stream to lower the RNG selling price. Solid-liquid separation is often preferred for easy handling and nutrient management if the digestate is sold as a fertilizer replacement. The liquid portion of the digestate is rich in nitrogen, whereas the solid portion is mostly phosphorus. Using digestate as fer- tilizer often requires additional processing to reduce pathogens, heavy metals, antibiotics used for animals, and other organic pollutants that might contaminate water and soil [69]. Moreover, excess nutrient sup- ply is a major challenge for sustainable agricultural practice. Applying unprocessed digestate for agriculture is not permitted in some EU countries, considering the risk of soil and water contaminations [7071]. In that case, getting rid of digestate can be an additional operational expense. The disposal costs depend on the location, soil nutrient con- ditions, available technologies, and governmental regulations for the disposal [72].

In this study, we have not considered any digestate disposal costs due a wide variation in available data. However, the minimum fuel selling price without digestate selling credits has been shown. For the base case scenario, $40/Mg and $2.64/Mg are considered the selling price of solid and liquid digestate, respectively [29].

The digester heat demand is provided by raw biogas combustion in a CHP (using a stationary internal combustion engine). However, natural from the grid is required to supply the start-up heat. Digester heat loss
Variable cost assumptions for the base case of manure anaerobic digestion to renewable natural gas.

Parameters|Value Plant financing by equity/debt|40%/60% of total capital investment Plant life|20 years Availability factor|85% Income tax rate|40% Interest rate for debt financing|7.5% Term of debt financing|10 years Working capital cost|5% of Fixed Capital Investment Depreciation schedule|7 years Construction period (spending schedule)|2.5 years (8% Y1, %Y2, 32 %Y3) Plant salvage value|None Start-up time|1 year Revenue and costs during start-up|Revenue = 50% of normal Revenue and costs during start-up|Variable cost = 75% of normal Revenue and costs during start-up|Fixed cost = 100% of normal

through walls and piping is also added to the total heat demand. The electricity for the pump, mixer, valves, and other ancillaries is assumed to be 12 kWh/Mg of feedstock [73]. Electricity demand for the com- pressors and pumps of the water scrubbing process and RNG compres- sion is calculated from the simulation model. The unit electricity price is 9.08 cents/kWh, based on the average retail price in Iowa in 2019 [74].

The US EPA assigns RIN (renewable identification number) credits to each gallon of renewable fuel. RNG produced from animal manure qualifies for D3 RIN credits (specific to cellulosic biofuel production). RINs can be sold in the market as a commodity to the obligated parties who need to blend a specific volume of renewable fuels, and the market price can vary widely over time. A $1.59/RIN is chosen as the base price for this study (November 2019) [75]. Another potential incentive is low carbon fuel standard (LCFS) credits administered by the California Air Resource Board (CARB) for supplying low carbon transportation fuel to California [76]. One LCFS credit is assigned to the producer for reducing 1 Mg of CO2 eq compared to the fossil-based fuel it replaces. CARB- approved dairy manure-derived RNG pathways have a wide variation in CI scores ranging from -177.03 to -532.74 [77]. The variations in CI score results from various factors such as feedstock production and conversion processes, utilization of byproducts, pipeline distance from the facility to California, business-as-us-usual manure management prac- tice, etc. To calculate the LCFS credit, this study assumes a CI score of -308.48, similar to an approved manure-derived RNG production project in Wisconsin [77]. As our cradle-to-gate LCA shows a 32% reduction in GHG emissions for the "with biochar" scenario, we consider the CI score 32% lower than the "without biochar" scenario. The LCFS credit price used in this study is $190.4/MT, the average price in 2021 (till October) [78]. Please note that this pathway has not been approved by CARB yet.

Fixed operating costs include annual employee salaries, overhead, maintenance, insurance, and taxes. The employee salaries are obtained from the Bureau of Labor Statistics [79]. The overhead expenses are assumed to be 5% of the total annual salaries. Annual maintenance and insurance costs are 2% of the total purchased equipment costs.

The project lifetime is considered 20 years with a 2.5-year con- struction period. Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS) is adopted to account for the capital depreciation for seven years of depreciation period. The key financial assumptions are listed in Table 6.

The goal of the LCA is to compare the environmental impact of beef manure-derived RNG for the "without biochar" and "with biochar" scenario, based on the attributional analysis. However, it is obvious that an increase in CH4 production due to biochar addition, on their own, would reduce emissions per functional unit.

We assume that the environmental benefits of biochar for soil application would be added to the benefit of manure digestate appli- cation. The N content of biochar could replace an equivalent amount of N fertilizer. However, most N would not be bioavailable due to the lower amount (0.5%) and its incalcitrant nature [15]. It has been reported that

biochar application reduces soil N2O emission and ammonium leaching in the long run due to the sorption capacity of biochar surfaces [84]. Biochar application can reduce 50% soil N2O emission from N fertilizer application [38]. Due to a lack of experimental data, a 1% reduction in soil N2O emission is considered for this study only to include the effect of biochar. This assumption is highly contingent on the biochar amount in digestate.

The significant contribution of biochar application is the sequestra- tion of biomass C into the soil, which was absorbed from the atmo- sphere. Due to biochar's molecular structure, most C becomes chemically and biologically stable and does not release CO2 over a long period (100-1000 years) when applied into the soil. For this study, 80% of the biochar C is considered stable, and the remaining 20% is emitted within the first year [38]. Moreover, the biochar conversion process (pyrolysis) produces syngas that replaces fossil natural gas for heating application. Further information on the LCI data is provided in the Supplementary Information.
39: Operating costs are categorized as variable operating costs and fixed operating costs. Variable operating costs depend on the production volume, whereas the fixed operating costs are constant throughout the
Fig. 3. Process flow diagram of high-pressure water scrubbing (HPWS) process.

operation period regardless of the production volume. Variable oper- ating costs include feedstock costs, consumables/chemical costs, utility (electricity, heating) costs, and feedstock transport costs. All variable costs are taken from a previous study [29] except biochar, electricity, and Renewable Identification Number (RIN) credits, as listed in Table 5. The only feedstock in this study is manure which is assumed to be purchased at $5/Mg [29]. Some researchers assumed zero costs for on- farm manure [54], and in many cases, tipping fees are assigned for processing manure from other farms [61]. The manure transport costs are not considered in the variable operating costs assuming on-farm plant location.

Biochar costs vary widely depending on the feedstock type, avail- ability, pyrolysis technology, and other region-specific parameters such as soil quality, crop production, biochar market, salary scale, [62]. No updated database is available to get the latest biochar market price for any region. In literature, various ranges are reported for a Mg of biochar such as $228-$280 (Poultry litter, Fast Pyrolysis-Gasifier) [63], $474-$704 (Wood, Auger pyrolysis) [64], $220-$280 (Pinewood, slow pyrolysis) [65], $75-248 (Rice, Fast pyrolysis), $680-1272 (Peanut, fast pyrolysis) [62], $218-274 (manure, mixed fast and slow pyrolysis) [66]. In their study, based on a European context, Arias et al. Field [27] considered a significantly higher biochar price ($546 per Mg 1). Brown et al. [67] estimated the minimum selling price of corn stover biochar for Iowa as $346/Mg biochar. Li et al. [62] provided an estimated price range between $87 and $250 for the same region. For this study, we consider the average of the higher range price estimate for corn stover biochar as $298/Mg. The price includes 15 km of transport costs of biochar from the pyrolysis facility to the RNG plant. A detailed cost analysis for biochar production is beyond this study's scope and is covered in detail in various previous articles [62,67,68].

The digestate selling price is a significant revenue stream to lower the RNG selling price. Solid-liquid separation is often preferred for easy handling and nutrient management if the digestate is sold as a fertilizer replacement. The liquid portion of the digestate is rich in nitrogen, whereas the solid portion is mostly phosphorus. Using digestate as fer- tilizer often requires additional processing to reduce pathogens, heavy metals, antibiotics used for animals, and other organic pollutants that might contaminate water and soil [69]. Moreover, excess nutrient sup- ply is a major challenge for sustainable agricultural practice. Applying unprocessed digestate for agriculture is not permitted in some EU countries, considering the risk of soil and water contaminations [7071]. In that case, getting rid of digestate can be an additional operational expense. The disposal costs depend on the location, soil nutrient con- ditions, available technologies, and governmental regulations for the disposal [72].

In this study, we have not considered any digestate disposal costs due a wide variation in available data. However, the minimum fuel selling price without digestate selling credits has been shown. For the base case scenario, $40/Mg and $2.64/Mg are considered the selling price of solid and liquid digestate, respectively [29].

The digester heat demand is provided by raw biogas combustion in a CHP (using a stationary internal combustion engine). However, natural from the grid is required to supply the start-up heat. Digester heat loss
Variable cost assumptions for the base case of manure anaerobic digestion to renewable natural gas.

Parameters|Value Plant financing by equity/debt|40%/60% of total capital investment Plant life|20 years Availability factor|85% Income tax rate|40% Interest rate for debt financing|7.5% Term of debt financing|10 years Working capital cost|5% of Fixed Capital Investment Depreciation schedule|7 years Construction period (spending schedule)|2.5 years (8% Y1, %Y2, 32 %Y3) Plant salvage value|None Start-up time|1 year Revenue and costs during start-up|Revenue = 50% of normal Revenue and costs during start-up|Variable cost = 75% of normal Revenue and costs during start-up|Fixed cost = 100% of normal

through walls and piping is also added to the total heat demand. The electricity for the pump, mixer, valves, and other ancillaries is assumed to be 12 kWh/Mg of feedstock [73]. Electricity demand for the com- pressors and pumps of the water scrubbing process and RNG compres- sion is calculated from the simulation model. The unit electricity price is 9.08 cents/kWh, based on the average retail price in Iowa in 2019 [74].

The US EPA assigns RIN (renewable identification number) credits to each gallon of renewable fuel. RNG produced from animal manure qualifies for D3 RIN credits (specific to cellulosic biofuel production). RINs can be sold in the market as a commodity to the obligated parties who need to blend a specific volume of renewable fuels, and the market price can vary widely over time. A $1.59/RIN is chosen as the base price for this study (November 2019) [75]. Another potential incentive is low carbon fuel standard (LCFS) credits administered by the California Air Resource Board (CARB) for supplying low carbon transportation fuel to California [76]. One LCFS credit is assigned to the producer for reducing 1 Mg of CO2 eq compared to the fossil-based fuel it replaces. CARB- approved dairy manure-derived RNG pathways have a wide variation in CI scores ranging from -177.03 to -532.74 [77]. The variations in CI score results from various factors such as feedstock production and conversion processes, utilization of byproducts, pipeline distance from the facility to California, business-as-us-usual manure management prac- tice, etc. To calculate the LCFS credit, this study assumes a CI score of -308.48, similar to an approved manure-derived RNG production project in Wisconsin [77]. As our cradle-to-gate LCA shows a 32% reduction in GHG emissions for the "with biochar" scenario, we consider the CI score 32% lower than the "without biochar" scenario. The LCFS credit price used in this study is $190.4/MT, the average price in 2021 (till October) [78]. Please note that this pathway has not been approved by CARB yet.

Fixed operating costs include annual employee salaries, overhead, maintenance, insurance, and taxes. The employee salaries are obtained from the Bureau of Labor Statistics [79]. The overhead expenses are assumed to be 5% of the total annual salaries. Annual maintenance and insurance costs are 2% of the total purchased equipment costs.

The project lifetime is considered 20 years with a 2.5-year con- struction period. Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS) is adopted to account for the capital depreciation for seven years of depreciation period. The key financial assumptions are listed in Table 6.

The goal of the LCA is to compare the environmental impact of beef manure-derived RNG for the "without biochar" and "with biochar" scenario, based on the attributional analysis. The guidelines provided by ISO 14040, 14,044 are followed [80,81]. Fig. 4 shows the system boundaries of the considered pathways with their inputs and outputs. All

Material|Costs Manure|$5/Mg Biochar|$298/Mg Solid digestate handling|$5/Mg Solid digested credit|$40/Mg Liquid digestate credit|$2.64/Mg Electricity|$0.09.08 RIN credits|$1.59/RIN LCFS credits|$190.4/credit

utilized inventory data are specific to the USA if not mentioned other- wise. The functional unit is defined as 1 MJ of RNG produced.

Following the cradle-to-gate approach, this study includes all emis- sions related to raw material extraction, transportation to plant, in-plant conversion, and delivery to the distribution point. The life cycle stages of the "without biochar" scenario include manure digestion, digestate transportation and application, biogas upgrading, RNG compression, and pipeline injection. The "with biochar" scenario includes biochar integrations related to lifecycle stages such as corn stover collection,
preprocessing, pyrolysis, and biochar application. The system bound- aries do not include emissions from capital goods, direct/indirect land- use change, and downstream RNG combustion.

2.4.2. Life cycle inventory (LCI) Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) data are collected from the USLCI database [82], published peer-reviewed literature, and process simulation model. LCA software SimaPro TM [28] is used to create the production system of RNG from beef manure. Two types of GHG emissions are considered for

the analysis: i) direct emissions (emissions produced from on-site ac- tivities and transportation), ii) indirect emissions (emissions produced from upstream or downstream processes outside the system boundary such as utilities).

2.4.2.1. Direct and indirect emission for manure only digestion. Direct emissions from an AD-RNG plant generally occur from fuel combustion for the digester start-up, biogas combustion for providing heat- electricity, biogas upgrading process, manure and digestate trans- portation, and digestate application machinery and soil after digestate application. Biogas combustion is generally considered carbon neutral as the emitted carbon was previously absorbed from the atmosphere. However, as we have fully credited the avoided emissions from con- ventional manure management, biogas combustion is considered a positive emission to avoid double counting. Fugitive CH4 emissions during AD and upgrading stages are considered 2% of the CH4 produced [73].

AD digestate applied to the soil emits CO2 and N20. In this study, all assumed parameters for CO2 and N2O emissions from digestate appli- cation are similar to Han et al. [73]. 62% of digestate C is assumed to be converted into CO2 over a 100-year time horizon, and the rest remains stored in the soil. N2O emissions occur via initrification-denitrification nitrate volatilization, and leaching/runoff to the water streams. N loss due to volatilization and leaching/runoff is considered to be 43% and 0.6%, respectively, similar to an anaerobic lagoon. N2O emission factors for both cases are 0.010 kg N2O-N/N loss. Indirect emissions include off- site production of electricity, natural gas, and diesel.

2.4.2.2. Avoided emissions for manure only digestion. Manure is usually collected from the farm, stored in a lagoon, and subsequently applied to the agricultural field. Emitted GHGs during manure storage are either burned or vented to the atmosphere, which is avoided if used for AD. The avoided emissions from the traditional manure management system are added as the credit, taken from Aui et al. [29]. The accounting of avoided emissions is in line with guidelines followed by government environmental agencies such as CARB [77] and the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).
The system expansion method is adopted to account for the digestate application. Synthetic N fertilizer is assumed to be replaced by the digestate N content.

2.4.2.3. Direct and indirect emissions related to biochar integration. Direct emissions result from corn stover collection, transportation, preprocess- ing, pyrolysis, biochar transportation to plant, and field transport. Emissions for corn stover collection and preprocessing are obtained from the GREET database based on ref [83]. A 15-km distance is assumed for the corn stover transport to the pyrolysis plant. The process parameters for an industrial-scale slow pyrolysis facility of corn stover are obtained from the study of Roberts et al. [38]. Material-energy balance and all process parameters are provided in the Supplementary Information. Emissions for biochar transportation for a 15-km travel radius are insignificant (9.64 X 10-6 kg CO2 eq/MJ of RNG) and hence excluded from the analysis. Indirect emissions include off-site natural gas, elec- tricity, and diesel production.

2.4.2.4. Avoided emissions for biochar integrated digestion. To the best of our knowledge, there is no published LCA data on the effect of biochar addition in AD for RNG production from manure. However, it is obvious that an increase in CH4 production due to biochar addition, on their own, would reduce emissions per functional unit.

We assume that the environmental benefits of biochar for soil application would be added to the benefit of manure digestate appli- cation. The N content of biochar could replace an equivalent amount of N fertilizer. However, most N would not be bioavailable due to the lower amount (0.5%) and its incalcitrant nature [15]. It has been reported that

biochar application reduces soil N2O emission and ammonium leaching in the long run due to the sorption capacity of biochar surfaces [84]. Biochar application can reduce 50% soil N2O emission from N fertilizer application [38]. Due to a lack of experimental data, a 1% reduction in soil N2O emission is considered for this study only to include the effect of biochar. This assumption is highly contingent on the biochar amount in digestate.

The significant contribution of biochar application is the sequestra- tion of biomass C into the soil, which was absorbed from the atmo- sphere. Due to biochar's molecular structure, most C becomes chemically and biologically stable and does not release CO2 over a long period (100-1000 years) when applied into the soil. For this study, 80% of the biochar C is considered stable, and the remaining 20% is emitted within the first year [38]. Moreover, the biochar conversion process (pyrolysis) produces syngas that replaces fossil natural gas for heating application. Further information on the LCI data is provided in the Supplementary Information.

Parameters|Value Plant financing by equity/debt|40%/60% of total capital investment Plant life|20 years Availability factor|85% Income tax rate|40% Interest rate for debt financing|7.5% Term of debt financing|10 years Working capital cost|5% of Fixed Capital Investment Depreciation schedule|7 years Construction period (spending schedule)|2.5 years (8% Y1, %Y2, 32 %Y3) Plant salvage value|None Start-up time|1 year Revenue and costs during start-up|Revenue = 50% of normal Revenue and costs during start-up|Variable cost = 75% of normal Revenue and costs during start-up|Fixed cost = 100% of normal

through walls and piping is also added to the total heat demand. The electricity for the pump, mixer, valves, and other ancillaries is assumed to be 12 kWh/Mg of feedstock [73]. Electricity demand for the com- pressors and pumps of the water scrubbing process and RNG compres- sion is calculated from the simulation model. The unit electricity price is 9.08 cents/kWh, based on the average retail price in Iowa in 2019 [74].

The US EPA assigns RIN (renewable identification number) credits to each gallon of renewable fuel. RNG produced from animal manure qualifies for D3 RIN credits (specific to cellulosic biofuel production). RINs can be sold in the market as a commodity to the obligated parties who need to blend a specific volume of renewable fuels, and the market price can vary widely over time. A $1.59/RIN is chosen as the base price for this study (November 2019) [75]. Another potential incentive is low carbon fuel standard (LCFS) credits administered by the California Air Resource Board (CARB) for supplying low carbon transportation fuel to California [76]. One LCFS credit is assigned to the producer for reducing 1 Mg of CO2 eq compared to the fossil-based fuel it replaces. CARB- approved dairy manure-derived RNG pathways have a wide variation in CI scores ranging from -177.03 to -532.74 [77]. The variations in CI score results from various factors such as feedstock production and conversion processes, utilization of byproducts, pipeline distance from the facility to California, business-as-us-usual manure management prac- tice, etc. To calculate the LCFS credit, this study assumes a CI score of -308.48, similar to an approved manure-derived RNG production project in Wisconsin [77]. As our cradle-to-gate LCA shows a 32% reduction in GHG emissions for the "with biochar" scenario, we consider the CI score 32% lower than the "without biochar" scenario. The LCFS credit price used in this study is $190.4/MT, the average price in 2021 (till October) [78]. Please note that this pathway has not been approved by CARB yet.

Fixed operating costs include annual employee salaries, overhead, maintenance, insurance, and taxes. The employee salaries are obtained from the Bureau of Labor Statistics [79]. The overhead expenses are assumed to be 5% of the total annual salaries. Annual maintenance and insurance costs are 2% of the total purchased equipment costs.

The project lifetime is considered 20 years with a 2.5-year con- struction period. Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS) is adopted to account for the capital depreciation for seven years of depreciation period. The key financial assumptions are listed in Table 6.

The goal of the LCA is to compare the environmental impact of beef manure-derived RNG for the "without biochar" and "with biochar" scenario, based on the attributional analysis. The guidelines provided by ISO 14040, 14,044 are followed [80,81]. Fig. 4 shows the system boundaries of the considered pathways with their inputs and outputs. All

Material|Costs Manure|$5/Mg Biochar|$298/Mg Solid digestate handling|$5/Mg Solid digested credit|$40/Mg Liquid digestate credit|$2.64/Mg Electricity|$0.09.08 RIN credits|$1.59/RIN LCFS credits|$190.4/credit
Fig. 4. System boundary of RNG production from animal manure, a) without biochar, b) with biochar (avoided processes are marked by dashed lines).
utilized inventory data are specific to the USA if not mentioned other- wise. The functional unit is defined as 1 MJ of RNG produced.

Following the cradle-to-gate approach, this study includes all emis- sions related to raw material extraction, transportation to plant, in-plant conversion, and delivery to the distribution point. The life cycle stages of the "without biochar" scenario include manure digestion, digestate transportation and application, biogas upgrading, RNG compression, and pipeline injection. The "with biochar" scenario includes biochar integrations related to lifecycle stages such as corn stover collection,
preprocessing, pyrolysis, and biochar application. The system bound- aries do not include emissions from capital goods, direct/indirect land- use change, and downstream RNG combustion.

2.4.2. Life cycle inventory (LCI) Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) data are collected from the USLCI database [82], published peer-reviewed literature, and process simulation model. LCA software SimaPro TM [28] is used to create the production system of RNG from beef manure. Two types of GHG emissions are considered for

the analysis: i) direct emissions (emissions produced from on-site ac- tivities and transportation), ii) indirect emissions (emissions produced from upstream or downstream processes outside the system boundary such as utilities).

2.4.2.1. Direct and indirect emission for manure only digestion. Direct emissions from an AD-RNG plant generally occur from fuel combustion for the digester start-up, biogas combustion for providing heat- electricity, biogas upgrading process, manure and digestate trans- portation, and digestate application machinery and soil after digestate application. Biogas combustion is generally considered carbon neutral as the emitted carbon was previously absorbed from the atmosphere. However, as we have fully credited the avoided emissions from con- ventional manure management, biogas combustion is considered a positive emission to avoid double counting. Fugitive CH4 emissions during AD and upgrading stages are considered 2% of the CH4 produced [73].

AD digestate applied to the soil emits CO2 and N20. In this study, all assumed parameters for CO2 and N2O emissions from digestate appli- cation are similar to Han et al. [73]. 62% of digestate C is assumed to be converted into CO2 over a 100-year time horizon, and the rest remains stored in the soil. N2O emissions occur via initrification-denitrification nitrate volatilization, and leaching/runoff to the water streams. N loss due to volatilization and leaching/runoff is considered to be 43% and 0.6%, respectively, similar to an anaerobic lagoon. N2O emission factors for both cases are 0.010 kg N2O-N/N loss. Indirect emissions include off- site production of electricity, natural gas, and diesel.

2.4.2.2. Avoided emissions for manure only digestion. Manure is usually collected from the farm, stored in a lagoon, and subsequently applied to the agricultural field. Emitted GHGs during manure storage are either burned or vented to the atmosphere, which is avoided if used for AD. The avoided emissions from the traditional manure management system are added as the credit, taken from Aui et al. [29]. The accounting of avoided emissions is in line with guidelines followed by government environmental agencies such as CARB [77] and the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).
The system expansion method is adopted to account for the digestate application. Synthetic N fertilizer is assumed to be replaced by the digestate N content.

2.4.2.3. Direct and indirect emissions related to biochar integration. Direct emissions result from corn stover collection, transportation, preprocess- ing, pyrolysis, biochar transportation to plant, and field transport. Emissions for corn stover collection and preprocessing are obtained from the GREET database based on ref [83]. A 15-km distance is assumed for the corn stover transport to the pyrolysis plant. The process parameters for an industrial-scale slow pyrolysis facility of corn stover are obtained from the study of Roberts et al. [38]. Material-energy balance and all process parameters are provided in the Supplementary Information. Emissions for biochar transportation for a 15-km travel radius are insignificant (9.64 X 10-6 kg CO2 eq/MJ of RNG) and hence excluded from the analysis. Indirect emissions include off-site natural gas, elec- tricity, and diesel production.

2.4.2.4. Avoided emissions for biochar integrated digestion. To the best of our knowledge, there is no published LCA data on the effect of biochar addition in AD for RNG production from manure. However, it is obvious that an increase in CH4 production due to biochar addition, on their own, would reduce emissions per functional unit.

We assume that the environmental benefits of biochar for soil application would be added to the benefit of manure digestate appli- cation. The N content of biochar could replace an equivalent amount of N fertilizer. However, most N would not be bioavailable due to the lower amount (0.5%) and its incalcitrant nature [15]. It has been reported that

biochar application reduces soil N2O emission and ammonium leaching in the long run due to the sorption capacity of biochar surfaces [84]. Biochar application can reduce 50% soil N2O emission from N fertilizer application [38]. Due to a lack of experimental data, a 1% reduction in soil N2O emission is considered for this study only to include the effect of biochar. This assumption is highly contingent on the biochar amount in digestate.

The significant contribution of biochar application is the sequestra- tion of biomass C into the soil, which was absorbed from the atmo- sphere. Due to biochar's molecular structure, most C becomes chemically and biologically stable and does not release CO2 over a long period (100-1000 years) when applied into the soil. For this study, 80% of the biochar C is considered stable, and the remaining 20% is emitted within the first year [38]. Moreover, the biochar conversion process (pyrolysis) produces syngas that replaces fossil natural gas for heating application. Further information on the LCI data is provided in the Supplementary Information.
42: Operating costs are categorized as variable operating costs and fixed operating costs. Variable operating costs depend on the production volume, whereas the fixed operating costs are constant throughout the
Fig. 3. Process flow diagram of high-pressure water scrubbing (HPWS) process.

operation period regardless of the production volume. Variable oper- ating costs include feedstock costs, consumables/chemical costs, utility (electricity, heating) costs, and feedstock transport costs. All variable costs are taken from a previous study [29] except biochar, electricity, and Renewable Identification Number (RIN) credits, as listed in Table 5. The only feedstock in this study is manure which is assumed to be purchased at $5/Mg [29]. Some researchers assumed zero costs for on- farm manure [54], and in many cases, tipping fees are assigned for processing manure from other farms [61]. The manure transport costs are not considered in the variable operating costs assuming on-farm plant location.

Biochar costs vary widely depending on the feedstock type, avail- ability, pyrolysis technology, and other region-specific parameters such as soil quality, crop production, biochar market, salary scale, [62]. No updated database is available to get the latest biochar market price for any region. In literature, various ranges are reported for a Mg of biochar such as $228-$280 (Poultry litter, Fast Pyrolysis-Gasifier) [63], $474-$704 (Wood, Auger pyrolysis) [64], $220-$280 (Pinewood, slow pyrolysis) [65], $75-248 (Rice, Fast pyrolysis), $680-1272 (Peanut, fast pyrolysis) [62], $218-274 (manure, mixed fast and slow pyrolysis) [66]. In their study, based on a European context, Arias et al. Field [27] considered a significantly higher biochar price ($546 per Mg 1). Brown et al. [67] estimated the minimum selling price of corn stover biochar for Iowa as $346/Mg biochar. Li et al. [62] provided an estimated price range between $87 and $250 for the same region. For this study, we consider the average of the higher range price estimate for corn stover biochar as $298/Mg. The price includes 15 km of transport costs of biochar from the pyrolysis facility to the RNG plant. A detailed cost analysis for biochar production is beyond this study's scope and is covered in detail in various previous articles [62,67,68].

The digestate selling price is a significant revenue stream to lower the RNG selling price. Solid-liquid separation is often preferred for easy handling and nutrient management if the digestate is sold as a fertilizer replacement. The liquid portion of the digestate is rich in nitrogen, whereas the solid portion is mostly phosphorus. Using digestate as fer- tilizer often requires additional processing to reduce pathogens, heavy metals, antibiotics used for animals, and other organic pollutants that might contaminate water and soil [69]. Moreover, excess nutrient sup- ply is a major challenge for sustainable agricultural practice. Applying unprocessed digestate for agriculture is not permitted in some EU countries, considering the risk of soil and water contaminations [7071]. In that case, getting rid of digestate can be an additional operational expense. The disposal costs depend on the location, soil nutrient con- ditions, available technologies, and governmental regulations for the disposal [72].

In this study, we have not considered any digestate disposal costs due a wide variation in available data. However, the minimum fuel selling price without digestate selling credits has been shown. For the base case scenario, $40/Mg and $2.64/Mg are considered the selling price of solid and liquid digestate, respectively [29].

The digester heat demand is provided by raw biogas combustion in a CHP (using a stationary internal combustion engine). However, natural from the grid is required to supply the start-up heat. Digester heat loss

the analysis: i) direct emissions (emissions produced from on-site ac- tivities and transportation), ii) indirect emissions (emissions produced from upstream or downstream processes outside the system boundary such as utilities).

2.4.2.1. Direct and indirect emission for manure only digestion. Direct emissions from an AD-RNG plant generally occur from fuel combustion for the digester start-up, biogas combustion for providing heat- electricity, biogas upgrading process, manure and digestate trans- portation, and digestate application machinery and soil after digestate application. Biogas combustion is generally considered carbon neutral as the emitted carbon was previously absorbed from the atmosphere. However, as we have fully credited the avoided emissions from con- ventional manure management, biogas combustion is considered a positive emission to avoid double counting. Fugitive CH4 emissions during AD and upgrading stages are considered 2% of the CH4 produced [73].

AD digestate applied to the soil emits CO2 and N20. In this study, all assumed parameters for CO2 and N2O emissions from digestate appli- cation are similar to Han et al. [73]. 62% of digestate C is assumed to be converted into CO2 over a 100-year time horizon, and the rest remains stored in the soil. N2O emissions occur via initrification-denitrification nitrate volatilization, and leaching/runoff to the water streams. N loss due to volatilization and leaching/runoff is considered to be 43% and 0.6%, respectively, similar to an anaerobic lagoon. N2O emission factors for both cases are 0.010 kg N2O-N/N loss. Indirect emissions include off- site production of electricity, natural gas, and diesel.

2.4.2.2. Avoided emissions for manure only digestion. Manure is usually collected from the farm, stored in a lagoon, and subsequently applied to the agricultural field. Emitted GHGs during manure storage are either burned or vented to the atmosphere, which is avoided if used for AD. The avoided emissions from the traditional manure management system are added as the credit, taken from Aui et al. [29]. The accounting of avoided emissions is in line with guidelines followed by government environmental agencies such as CARB [77] and the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).
The system expansion method is adopted to account for the digestate application. Synthetic N fertilizer is assumed to be replaced by the digestate N content.

2.4.2.3. Direct and indirect emissions related to biochar integration. Direct emissions result from corn stover collection, transportation, preprocess- ing, pyrolysis, biochar transportation to plant, and field transport. Emissions for corn stover collection and preprocessing are obtained from the GREET database based on ref [83]. A 15-km distance is assumed for the corn stover transport to the pyrolysis plant. The process parameters for an industrial-scale slow pyrolysis facility of corn stover are obtained from the study of Roberts et al. [38]. Material-energy balance and all process parameters are provided in the Supplementary Information. Emissions for biochar transportation for a 15-km travel radius are insignificant (9.64 X 10-6 kg CO2 eq/MJ of RNG) and hence excluded from the analysis. Indirect emissions include off-site natural gas, elec- tricity, and diesel production.

2.4.2.4. Avoided emissions for biochar integrated digestion. To the best of our knowledge, there is no published LCA data on the effect of biochar addition in AD for RNG production from manure. However, it is obvious that an increase in CH4 production due to biochar addition, on their own, would reduce emissions per functional unit.

We assume that the environmental benefits of biochar for soil application would be added to the benefit of manure digestate appli- cation. The N content of biochar could replace an equivalent amount of N fertilizer. However, most N would not be bioavailable due to the lower amount (0.5%) and its incalcitrant nature [15]. It has been reported that

biochar application reduces soil N2O emission and ammonium leaching in the long run due to the sorption capacity of biochar surfaces [84]. Biochar application can reduce 50% soil N2O emission from N fertilizer application [38]. Due to a lack of experimental data, a 1% reduction in soil N2O emission is considered for this study only to include the effect of biochar. This assumption is highly contingent on the biochar amount in digestate.

The significant contribution of biochar application is the sequestra- tion of biomass C into the soil, which was absorbed from the atmo- sphere. Due to biochar's molecular structure, most C becomes chemically and biologically stable and does not release CO2 over a long period (100-1000 years) when applied into the soil. For this study, 80% of the biochar C is considered stable, and the remaining 20% is emitted within the first year [38]. Moreover, the biochar conversion process (pyrolysis) produces syngas that replaces fossil natural gas for heating application. Further information on the LCI data is provided in the Supplementary Information.
44: Operating costs are categorized as variable operating costs and fixed operating costs. Variable operating costs depend on the production volume, whereas the fixed operating costs are constant throughout the
Fig. 3. Process flow diagram of high-pressure water scrubbing (HPWS) process.

operation period regardless of the production volume. Variable oper- ating costs include feedstock costs, consumables/chemical costs, utility (electricity, heating) costs, and feedstock transport costs. All variable costs are taken from a previous study [29] except biochar, electricity, and Renewable Identification Number (RIN) credits, as listed in Table 5. The only feedstock in this study is manure which is assumed to be purchased at $5/Mg [29]. Some researchers assumed zero costs for on- farm manure [54], and in many cases, tipping fees are assigned for processing manure from other farms [61]. The manure transport costs are not considered in the variable operating costs assuming on-farm plant location.

Biochar costs vary widely depending on the feedstock type, avail- ability, pyrolysis technology, and other region-specific parameters such as soil quality, crop production, biochar market, salary scale, [62]. No updated database is available to get the latest biochar market price for any region. In literature, various ranges are reported for a Mg of biochar such as $228-$280 (Poultry litter, Fast Pyrolysis-Gasifier) [63], $474-$704 (Wood, Auger pyrolysis) [64], $220-$280 (Pinewood, slow pyrolysis) [65], $75-248 (Rice, Fast pyrolysis), $680-1272 (Peanut, fast pyrolysis) [62], $218-274 (manure, mixed fast and slow pyrolysis) [66]. In their study, based on a European context, Arias et al. Field [27] considered a significantly higher biochar price ($546 per Mg 1). Brown et al. [67] estimated the minimum selling price of corn stover biochar for Iowa as $346/Mg biochar. Li et al. [62] provided an estimated price range between $87 and $250 for the same region. For this study, we consider the average of the higher range price estimate for corn stover biochar as $298/Mg. The price includes 15 km of transport costs of biochar from the pyrolysis facility to the RNG plant. A detailed cost analysis for biochar production is beyond this study's scope and is covered in detail in various previous articles [62,67,68].

The digestate selling price is a significant revenue stream to lower the RNG selling price. Solid-liquid separation is often preferred for easy handling and nutrient management if the digestate is sold as a fertilizer replacement. The liquid portion of the digestate is rich in nitrogen, whereas the solid portion is mostly phosphorus. Using digestate as fer- tilizer often requires additional processing to reduce pathogens, heavy metals, antibiotics used for animals, and other organic pollutants that might contaminate water and soil [69]. Moreover, excess nutrient sup- ply is a major challenge for sustainable agricultural practice. Applying unprocessed digestate for agriculture is not permitted in some EU countries, considering the risk of soil and water contaminations [7071]. In that case, getting rid of digestate can be an additional operational expense. The disposal costs depend on the location, soil nutrient con- ditions, available technologies, and governmental regulations for the disposal [72].

In this study, we have not considered any digestate disposal costs due a wide variation in available data. However, the minimum fuel selling price without digestate selling credits has been shown. For the base case scenario, $40/Mg and $2.64/Mg are considered the selling price of solid and liquid digestate, respectively [29].

The digester heat demand is provided by raw biogas combustion in a CHP (using a stationary internal combustion engine). However, natural from the grid is required to supply the start-up heat. Digester heat loss
Variable cost assumptions for the base case of manure anaerobic digestion to renewable natural gas.

Parameters|Value Plant financing by equity/debt|40%/60% of total capital investment Plant life|20 years Availability factor|85% Income tax rate|40% Interest rate for debt financing|7.5% Term of debt financing|10 years Working capital cost|5% of Fixed Capital Investment Depreciation schedule|7 years Construction period (spending schedule)|2.5 years (8% Y1, %Y2, 32 %Y3) Plant salvage value|None Start-up time|1 year Revenue and costs during start-up|Revenue = 50% of normal Revenue and costs during start-up|Variable cost = 75% of normal Revenue and costs during start-up|Fixed cost = 100% of normal

through walls and piping is also added to the total heat demand. The electricity for the pump, mixer, valves, and other ancillaries is assumed to be 12 kWh/Mg of feedstock [73]. Electricity demand for the com- pressors and pumps of the water scrubbing process and RNG compres- sion is calculated from the simulation model. The unit electricity price is 9.08 cents/kWh, based on the average retail price in Iowa in 2019 [74].

The US EPA assigns RIN (renewable identification number) credits to each gallon of renewable fuel. RNG produced from animal manure qualifies for D3 RIN credits (specific to cellulosic biofuel production). RINs can be sold in the market as a commodity to the obligated parties who need to blend a specific volume of renewable fuels, and the market price can vary widely over time. A $1.59/RIN is chosen as the base price for this study (November 2019) [75]. Another potential incentive is low carbon fuel standard (LCFS) credits administered by the California Air Resource Board (CARB) for supplying low carbon transportation fuel to California [76]. One LCFS credit is assigned to the producer for reducing 1 Mg of CO2 eq compared to the fossil-based fuel it replaces. CARB- approved dairy manure-derived RNG pathways have a wide variation in CI scores ranging from -177.03 to -532.74 [77]. The variations in CI score results from various factors such as feedstock production and conversion processes, utilization of byproducts, pipeline distance from the facility to California, business-as-us-usual manure management prac- tice, etc. To calculate the LCFS credit, this study assumes a CI score of -308.48, similar to an approved manure-derived RNG production project in Wisconsin [77]. As our cradle-to-gate LCA shows a 32% reduction in GHG emissions for the "with biochar" scenario, we consider the CI score 32% lower than the "without biochar" scenario. The LCFS credit price used in this study is $190.4/MT, the average price in 2021 (till October) [78]. Please note that this pathway has not been approved by CARB yet.

Fixed operating costs include annual employee salaries, overhead, maintenance, insurance, and taxes. The employee salaries are obtained from the Bureau of Labor Statistics [79]. The overhead expenses are assumed to be 5% of the total annual salaries. Annual maintenance and insurance costs are 2% of the total purchased equipment costs.

The project lifetime is considered 20 years with a 2.5-year con- struction period. Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS) is adopted to account for the capital depreciation for seven years of depreciation period. The key financial assumptions are listed in Table 6.

The goal of the LCA is to compare the environmental impact of beef manure-derived RNG for the "without biochar" and "with biochar" scenario, based on the attributional analysis. The guidelines provided by ISO 14040, 14,044 are followed [80,81]. Fig. 4 shows the system boundaries of the considered pathways with their inputs and outputs. All

Following the cradle-to-gate approach, this study includes all emis- sions related to raw material extraction, transportation to plant, in-plant conversion, and delivery to the distribution point. The life cycle stages of the "without biochar" scenario include manure digestion, digestate transportation and application, biogas upgrading, RNG compression, and pipeline injection. The "with biochar" scenario includes biochar integrations related to lifecycle stages such as corn stover collection,
preprocessing, pyrolysis, and biochar application. The system bound- aries do not include emissions from capital goods, direct/indirect land- use change, and downstream RNG combustion.

2.4.2. Life cycle inventory (LCI) Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) data are collected from the USLCI database [82], published peer-reviewed literature, and process simulation model. LCA software SimaPro TM [28] is used to create the production system of RNG from beef manure. Two types of GHG emissions are considered for

the analysis: i) direct emissions (emissions produced from on-site ac- tivities and transportation), ii) indirect emissions (emissions produced from upstream or downstream processes outside the system boundary such as utilities).

45: Life cycle impact assessment is conducted using the TRACI 2.4, a midpoint-oriented method developed by US EPA [85]. Global warming potential (GWP) is calculated by summing the GWP of each unit process from raw material extraction to RNG production. According to IPCC, GWP of CO2, CH4, and N2O are 1, 25, and 298 kg CO2 eq/kg-emission, respectively, for a 100-years time horizon.
46: Sensitivity and uncertainty analysis are useful tools for quantifying the probability of deviation from the base case results due to uncertainty in the model inputs. The sensitivity analysis reflects the contribution of a single parameter on the model output, whereas uncertainty analysis checks possible variations of all key parameters simultaneously. The literature review reveals that biogas enhancement during AD depends on the feedstock and biochar combination. The detailed kinetic modeling of the biochar amended digester is not yet available and difficult to generalize for the wide range of biochar sources and digester feedstocks. Therefore, sensitivity analysis of key process and economic parameters shows their relative influences on MSP.

The biogas enhancement during AD is the main metric to justify the additional costs of biochar addition. Literature review shows a wide range of variations in biogas enhancement from 14 to 70% [12,14,20,86]. The digester feed's optimum biochar dosage ranges be- tween 1 and 10% (wt.% of TS), depending on the substrate-biochar characteristics. Biochar price depends on the source, conversion tech- nology, process parameters, and location-specific factors. D3 RIN price varies like other commodities and can significantly influence MSP. The lowest price in the last three years ($0.59/RIN) and the average price in 2021 ($2/RIN) are considered for the pessimistic and optimistic cases, respectively [75]. The sensitivity analysis also investigates the influence of farm size, IRR, and digestate selling price. Parameters for LCA sensitivity include biogas increase, manure moisture content, manure N content, stable C in biochar, biochar dosage, and manure C sequestra- tion. Parameters used for the economic and environmental analysis are provided in Table 7.

The Monte Carlo simulation technique is employed for economic and environmental uncertainty analysis. This technique takes the distribu- tion of the chosen parameters to run mathematical models and provides statistical distributions of possible outcomes. Five thousand random values for each parameter are generated using a triangular distribution, where the minimum, maximum, and mode are equal to the pessimistic, optimistic, and base case in Table 7. Unique combinations of the generated parameter values are fed into the spreadsheet, and the calculated MSP and GHG emissions are collected. A probability distri- bution fitting to the results describes the range of possible MSP and GHG emissions within a 10-90% confidence interval. Python code is used to

50: Table 8 illustrates the studied pathway's mass-energy balance data and other process parameters. Based on the base case farm size (5000 heads), the digester input flow rate is calculated as 209 Mg/day. For the "with biochar" scenario, biochar is added at the rate of 1.24 Mg/day. The digester volume would remain unchanged as the additional biochar volume can be accommodated within the considered extra volume for the "without biochar" scenario.

The biogas potential from the simulation model is estimated as 0.302 Nm3 TS and 0.387 Nm3 /kg TS for the "without biochar" and "with biochar" scenarios, respectively. Wellinger et al. [87] reported manure biogas potential as 0.333 Nm3 /kg TS, comparable to our results. Total biogas production increases by 28% due to biochar addition for the base case. Biogas meets the entire plant heat demand (digester, HPWS) and simultaneously produces electricity. The total energy demand for biogas upgrading is 0.30 kWh/Nm3, where biogas compression consumes 0.25 kWh/Nm3 and the rest is for the water pumping and air blower. Liter- ature review shows energy consumption for HPWS ranges between 0.2 and 0.5 kWh/Nm³ of upgraded biogas [88].

On-site biogas consumptions are 15% and 12% of the produced raw biogas for the "without biochar" and "with biochar" scenarios, respec- tively. Although the proportion of consumed biogas is lower for the "with biochar" scenario, the net amount is higher due to higher feed- stock volume (for biochar addition) and additional raw biogas for

52: Table 8 illustrates the studied pathway's mass-energy balance data and other process parameters. Based on the base case farm size (5000 heads), the digester input flow rate is calculated as 209 Mg/day. For the "with biochar" scenario, biochar is added at the rate of 1.24 Mg/day. The digester volume would remain unchanged as the additional biochar volume can be accommodated within the considered extra volume for the "without biochar" scenario.

The biogas potential from the simulation model is estimated as 0.302 Nm3 TS and 0.387 Nm3 /kg TS for the "without biochar" and "with biochar" scenarios, respectively. Wellinger et al. [87] reported manure biogas potential as 0.333 Nm3 /kg TS, comparable to our results. Total biogas production increases by 28% due to biochar addition for the base case. Biogas meets the entire plant heat demand (digester, HPWS) and simultaneously produces electricity. The total energy demand for biogas upgrading is 0.30 kWh/Nm3, where biogas compression consumes 0.25 kWh/Nm3 and the rest is for the water pumping and air blower. Liter- ature review shows energy consumption for HPWS ranges between 0.2 and 0.5 kWh/Nm³ of upgraded biogas [88].

On-site biogas consumptions are 15% and 12% of the produced raw biogas for the "without biochar" and "with biochar" scenarios, respec- tively. Although the proportion of consumed biogas is lower for the "with biochar" scenario, the net amount is higher due to higher feed- stock volume (for biochar addition) and additional raw biogas for

54: Table 9 provides detailed economic results for RNG production from the AD of manure for the "without biochar" and "with biochar" sce- narios. Capital costs for the AD digester, including generator set, is $3.08 M. Biggar et al. [89] reported total capital costs for similar digester configurations as $3.18 M. The higher capital costs mainly resulted from the ten times larger generator size compared to this study. The capital costs of the AD unit are slightly higher for the "with biochar" scenario. The "without biochar" scenario requires 85 kW of the gener- ator, whereas the "with biochar" scenario requires 87 kW to generate additional digester heat. A 2% extra design allowance for the digester size would be sufficient to accommodate the added biochar.

The biochar integration facility includes biochar storage, an open- end loader for moving biochar to the AD section, and a conveyor belt feeding the digester. The estimated additional capital costs for biochar addition is $200,000, which is 2% of the total capital costs.

The gas upgrading section requires 15% higher capital costs for the "with biochar" scenario to process additional biogas produced due to biochar addition. RNG compression and injection unit account for approximately 32% of the total capital cost; however, there is no sig- nificant costs increase for additional RNG volume. Because the capital cost of the RNG injection point and pipeline construction depends only on the location, not on the produced volume, this provides a significant economy-of-scale benefit for the "with biochar" scenario where RNG production increases by 32%.

The capital cost distributions for different sections per GJ of RNG produced annually are shown in Fig. 5. As expected, capital costs per GJ for every section are lower for the "with biochar" scenario due to higher RNG production. Capital costs decrease by 24% for the AD (includes generator) and RNG compression-injection section. However, the capital

cost of biogas upgrading was reduced by 11%. Higher biogas yield re- quires higher capital costs for upgrading. Additionally, the economy of scale benefit is small for the mid-size biogas upgrading facility.

The production costs of the "without biochar" scenario are compared with the published literature data to validate the TEA model assump- tions. The TEA model estimates RNG production cost (excluding RNG compression, injection, byproduct credits) as $26.66/GJ without

biochar addition. Jaffe et al. [90] reported that tank digester-based (complete mix, plug flow) RNG production costs range between $15/ GJ to $32/GJ for different farm sizes. Kaufman et al. [91] show manure- based biomethane production costs may vary between $10 to $24 per GJ, based on plant scale and location. The model estimated biogas upgrading and pipeline injection costs (excluding AD) are $14.34/GJ. Reported upgrading and injection costs from various sources are listed

Without Biochar
With Biochar

[ ] Average return on Investment|[X] By Products Credits [ ] RIN Credits|[ ] Waste disposal [X] Average income tax|[ ] Consumables [ ] Capital Depreciation|[X] Utilities [X] Tax and insurance|[ ] Water [X] Maintenance|[X] BioChar [X] Overhead|[X] Manure [X] Labor|
Fig. 6. Operating costs distributions for renewable natural gas production with and without biochar (negative costs indicate credit).
by Kaufman et al. [91], and the interpolated costs for similar plant size (6554 Nm3 /day) are $16.29/GJ.

The operating costs include variable operating costs and fixed operating costs. The feedstock costs for the "with biochar" scenario in- crease by 36% for the biochar addition. A 24% increase in utility costs is mainly due to the gas cleaning section processing additional biogas after biochar integration. Cozma et al. [92] reported that higher methane concentration in biogas would reduce the water and energy requirement of the process. If the biochar addition performs in-situ CO2 removal according to Shen et al. [16], energy demand will decrease, reducing the operating costs. However, in-situ CO2 removal is not considered in this study. The consumable costs are assumed to be $0.00051/Nm 3 CH4, according to Sahoo et al. [54]. Labor and overhead costs are unchanged, considering that no extra employees are required for the biochar inte- gration. Maintenance and insurance costs increase by 9% and 8%, respectively, depending on the total project costs.

Fig. 6 provides a breakdown of operating costs and minimum selling price (MSP) for both scenarios. Compared to the "without biochar" scenario, the MSP decreases by 22% for the "with biochar" scenario. This is mainly due to increased RNG production and negligible change in additional input costs. Except for biochar costs, all items of the operating costs have lower costs per GJ. Only RIN credits are included as the environmental credits. The CO2 sequestration credits associated with biochar production are not considered here, assuming they are already included with biochar costs. Insurance and taxes slightly vary based on the capital cost difference.

Fig. 7 shows that digestate selling revenue lowers the MSP by 15% and 13% for the "without biochar" and "with biochar" scenarios. For the "with biochar" scenario, the solid content of the digestate is higher due to added biochar. Revenue from RIN credits lowers MSP by 43% and 48% for the 'without biochar" and "with biochar" scenario, respectively. Considering digestate revenue and RIN credits, the RNG selling price for the "with biochar" scenario is 22% lower than the "without biochar"

scenario. The LCFS credit is $73.47/GJ for the "without biochar" sce- nario and $92.17/GJ for the "with biochar" scenario. So, adding LCFS credits would significantly lower the MSP if it qualifies as a CARB- approved pathway. CO2 as the industrial feedstock might be another potential revenue source; however, wide variations in existing market prices are not considered in the model.

Insurance and taxes slightly vary based on the capital cost difference.

Fig. 7 shows that digestate selling revenue lowers the MSP by 15% and 13% for the "without biochar" and "with biochar" scenarios. For the "with biochar" scenario, the solid content of the digestate is higher due to added biochar. Revenue from RIN credits lowers MSP by 43% and 48% for the 'without biochar" and "with biochar" scenario, respectively. Considering digestate revenue and RIN credits, the RNG selling price for the "with biochar" scenario is 22% lower than the "without biochar"

scenario. The LCFS credit is $73.47/GJ for the "without biochar" sce- nario and $92.17/GJ for the "with biochar" scenario. So, adding LCFS credits would significantly lower the MSP if it qualifies as a CARB- approved pathway. CO2 as the industrial feedstock might be another potential revenue source; however, wide variations in existing market prices are not considered in the model.

The operating costs include variable operating costs and fixed operating costs. The feedstock costs for the "with biochar" scenario in- crease by 36% for the biochar addition. A 24% increase in utility costs is mainly due to the gas cleaning section processing additional biogas after biochar integration. Cozma et al. [92] reported that higher methane concentration in biogas would reduce the water and energy requirement of the process. If the biochar addition performs in-situ CO2 removal according to Shen et al. [16], energy demand will decrease, reducing the operating costs. However, in-situ CO2 removal is not considered in this study. The consumable costs are assumed to be $0.00051/Nm 3 CH4, according to Sahoo et al. [54]. Labor and overhead costs are unchanged, considering that no extra employees are required for the biochar inte- gration. Maintenance and insurance costs increase by 9% and 8%, respectively, depending on the total project costs.

Fig. 6 provides a breakdown of operating costs and minimum selling price (MSP) for both scenarios. 62: Fig. 8 (a-b) illustrates the net GHG emissions for 1 MJ of RNG pro- duced. GHG emissions for the "without biochar" scenario is -0.033 kg CO2 eq/MJ of RNG produced. Han et al. [73] reported -0.040 kg CO2 eq /MJ emissions for AD-based manure to RNG production, comparable to our result. GHG emissions are -0.043 kg CO2 eq /MJ of RNG produced for the "with biochar" scenario. So, the biochar addition reduces GHG emissions by 32%. Net negative emissions result from emission avoid- ance for both scenarios with additional carbon sequestration from bio- char application to soil.

Emissions occur from i) biogas combustion for heat and electricity production, ii) agricultural machinery for digestate application, iii) digestate after soil application, and iv) CH4 leakage during biogas pro- duction and upgrading stages for both scenarios. Biogas combustion provides heat for the AD digester to maintain temperature. Biochar addition reduces the amount of manure feed to produce 1 MJ of RNG, leading to a 29% emission reduction. After soil application, digestate emits 40% more CO2 and 29% more N2O for the "without biochar" scenario due to higher manure digestate production. For the same reason, digestate transportation and application emissions reduce by 26% for the "with biochar" scenario.

Emission avoidance credits from conventional manure management are included for both scenarios. The "without biochar" scenario provides 29% more manure emission avoidance credits than the "with biochar" scenario, as a lower amount of manure goes to the digester for the latter
Fig. 7. Variations of renewable natural gas minimum selling price based on digestate credits, RIN credits, and LCFS credits.

Fig. 8. GHG emissions at different lifecycle stages per MJ of RNG produced (a) without biochar, and (b) with biochar (Please note, emission from biogas combustion is biogenic and generally considered as zero. However, we have considered this positive to avoid double counting the benefits of avoided emissions as discussed in section 2.4.2).

case. Avoided products like natural gas (by syngas during biochar pro- duction) and electricity (during AD process) add credits to the total emission. Electricity production credit is 65% lower for the "with bio- char" scenario. Because biogas is combusted in the CHP to meet the plant heat requirement and electricity is produced as a byproduct. The "with biochar" scenario requires lower digester heat and produces less electricity.

Negative emissions are mainly due to the C sequestration with the digestate application and avoided synthetic fertilizer production. 38% of the digestate C is sequestered in the soil when applied in the crop field. The "with biochar" scenario sequesters additional C with the biochar; however, the benefit is mostly offset by the reduced amount of manure C sequestration. Note that the manure feed's biochar is only 5% (wt. % of TS). The C sequestration benefit will significantly increase for an increased amount of biochar. Syngas produced during pyrolysis replaces an equivalent amount of fossil-based natural gas, reducing additional emissions.

66: Fig. 9 shows the influence of different economic parameters on MSP. Farm size has the most significant impact, followed by the RIN price. Increasing farm size by 100% would decrease the MSP by 60%, whereas

reducing the size by 100% would increase the MSP by 101%. This shows a significant economy of scale for farm-based RNG production plants. RIN price seems to be a determinant factor for economic viability. For farm-based digester, manure cost can be assumed zero; however, for a conservative estimate, this study considers $5/Mg for the base case scenario. Manure costs may increase if the transportation costs are added for a centralized plant where manure is collected from different farm locations. The biogas increase due to biochar addition is an important influencing factor. A 70% increase in biogas yield lowers the MSP by 47% from the base case. Biochar amended digestate is consid- ered superior to manure-only digestate quality. A 50% increase in digestate selling price can lower the MSP by 8%. Biochar dosage and price have the least impact on the MSP due to the low biochar required.

Sensitivity analysis of GHG emissions for various parameter varia- tions is shown in Fig. 10. GHG emissions are mainly influenced by the manure moisture content followed by the biochar dosage. Manure moisture content determines the biochar dosage as the biochar is mixed in proportion to TS. The higher the TS, the higher the biochar require- ment. As the GHG reduction results mostly from biochar carbon sequestration and fossil energy displacement, the production of more biochar brings positive benefits. An increase in biogas yield implies less manure is required to produce 1 MJ of RNG; therefore, less C is sequestered with the digestate. Emission avoidance credits for avoiding
Change in minimum RNG selling price
Fig. 9. Sensitivity analysis of the renewable natural gas minimum selling price from biochar-amended manure anaerobic digestion.
Fig. 10. Sensitivity analysis of the greenhouse gas emissions for renewable natural gas production from biochar amended manure anaerobic digestion.
conventional manure management also decrease with increased biogas yield. Manure N content has the least influence on GHG emissions.

Fig. 11 (a) shows the probability distribution curve of the RNG MSP for the parameter variations (Table 7). A normal distribution curve is fitted to the set of possible outcomes. The mean MSP is $15.26/GJ with a standard deviation of $2.91. The MSP varies between $11.56/GJ and $19.02/GJ within a 10-90% confidence interval as shaded with green color.

Fig. 11 (b) shows the GHG abatement probability distribution curve for parameter variations (Table 7). The probability distribution follows a lognormal distribution. The mean GHG abatement is 0.06 kg CO2 eq/MJ with a standard deviation of 0.011 kg CO2 eq/MJ. The minimum and maximum GHG abatement can be 0.045 kg CO2 eq /MJ and 0.074 kg CO2 eq/MJ, respectively, within 10 and 90% confidence intervals (shaded with green color).
