For farm-based digester, manure cost can be assumed zero; however, for a conservative estimate, this study considers $5/Mg for the base case scenario. Manure costs may increase if the transportation costs are added for a centralized plant where manure is collected from different farm locations. The biogas increase due to biochar addition is an important influencing factor. A 70% increase in biogas yield lowers the MSP by 47% from the base case. Biochar amended digestate is consid- ered superior to manure-only digestate quality. A 50% increase in digestate selling price can lower the MSP by 8%. Biochar dosage and price have the least impact on the MSP due to the low biochar required.

Sensitivity analysis of GHG emissions for various parameter varia- tions is shown in Fig. 10. GHG emissions are mainly influenced by the manure moisture content followed by the biochar dosage. Manure moisture content determines the biochar dosage as the biochar is mixed in proportion to TS. The higher the TS, the higher the biochar require- ment. As the GHG reduction results mostly from biochar carbon sequestration and fossil energy displacement, the production of more biochar brings positive benefits. An increase in biogas yield implies less manure is required to produce 1 MJ of RNG; therefore, less C is sequestered with the digestate. Emission avoidance credits for avoiding
Change in minimum RNG selling price
Fig. 9. Sensitivity analysis of the renewable natural gas minimum selling price from biochar-amended manure anaerobic digestion.
Fig. 10. Sensitivity analysis of the greenhouse gas emissions for renewable natural gas production from biochar amended manure anaerobic digestion.
conventional manure management also decrease with increased biogas yield. Manure N content has the least influence on GHG emissions.

Fig. 11 (a) shows the probability distribution curve of the RNG MSP for the parameter variations (Table 7). A normal distribution curve is fitted to the set of possible outcomes. The mean MSP is $15.26/GJ with a standard deviation of $2.91. The MSP varies between $11.56/GJ and $19.02/GJ within a 10-90% confidence interval as shaded with green color.

Fig. 11 (b) shows the GHG abatement probability distribution curve for parameter variations (Table 7). The probability distribution follows a lognormal distribution. The mean GHG abatement is 0.06 kg CO2 eq/MJ with a standard deviation of 0.011 kg CO2 eq/MJ. The minimum and maximum GHG abatement can be 0.045 kg CO2 eq /MJ and 0.074 kg CO2 eq/MJ, respectively, within 10 and 90% confidence intervals (shaded with green color).
70: The positive influence of biochar on AD is evident in the literature. However, biogas yield and quality vary widely depending on the base substrate and biochar characteristics. This study assumes a base case scenario that can be significantly different for different substrate- biochar combinations. The main limitation of the process model is the validation with available experimental data. This study employs lab- scale experimental data that might differ for a scaled-up operation. The influence of seasonal ambient temperature variations on manure production is also not considered for the analysis.
Location-specific conditions can have a significant influence on the overall system viability. Our location-specific assumptions are based in

Iowa, USA, rich in agricultural farms and residues. Other locations might have different substrate and biochar sources available at different costs. The impact of biochar on the co-digestion of multiple feedstocks is not included in this study.

The MSP includes currently available government incentives such as RIN for the analysis. Any policy change can positively or negatively impact the economic viability of this pathway. For the incentive calcu- lations, various transaction fees are not considered.

Iowa, USA, rich in agricultural farms and residues. Other locations might have different substrate and biochar sources available at different costs. The impact of biochar on the co-digestion of multiple feedstocks is not included in this study.

The MSP includes currently available government incentives such as RIN for the analysis. Any policy change can positively or negatively impact the economic viability of this pathway. For the incentive calcu- lations, various transaction fees are not considered.
72: This study investigated corn stover biochar's techno-economic and environmental impact on RNG production. Techno-economic analysis showed that adding 5% (wt. % of manure TS) corn stover biochar could reduce the minimum RNG selling price by 15% for a 28% increase in the biogas yield. Digestate selling revenue and RIN credits can lower the RNG price by 22%. Biochar addition can increase the total capital costs by 8% and operating costs by 13%. Sensitivity analysis shows farm size has the largest influence on the RNG price, followed by the RIN price, while biochar price has the least influence. For small to mid-size farms, manure to RNG production would not be viable due to the high economy-of-scale of the upgrading facility. Investment costs for RNG compression and injection facility are also high; therefore, increasing RNG production would lower per-unit production costs leading to the market competitive selling price.

LCA results show 32% GHG abatement for the biochar addition scenario due to carbon sequestration and natural gas displacement. Other environmental benefits of biochar soil application are already proven to increase the selling price of digestate mixed with biochar.
Fig. 11. Uncertainty analysis of the biochar-amended manure anaerobic digestion to renewable natural gas a) minimum fuel selling price, and b) greenhouse gas emission abatement.

Uncertainty analysis shows the RNG selling price for the "with biochar" scenario varies between $11.56/GJ and $19.02/GJ within a 10-90% confidence interval, while GHG abatement can vary between 0.045 kg CO2 eq /MJ and 0.074 kg CO2 eq/MJ. It is difficult to generalize the economic gain from the biochar amended AD process unless a case-specific analysis is performed. Nevertheless, this study illustrates that the additional costs for biochar addition could be insignificant compared to the net benefit. However, federal and state-level incentives are still required to make RNG pro- duction from AD cost-competitive with fossil-based natural gas under current market conditions.
